topics.title: Introduction to Game Theory - Competitive Equilibrium, Rationality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST756 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Game Theory - Competitive Equilibrium, Rationality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Game Theory — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Game Theory** is the formal mathematical study of **strategic interaction** among **rational decision-makers (players)**. It models situations where the outcome for any individual player depends not only on his/her own actions but also on the actions chosen by other players.

Formally, a **non-cooperative game in strategic (normal) form** is a triple:
$$G = \langle N, (S_i)_{i \in N}, (u_i)_{i \in N} \rangle$$

where:
- $N = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ is the finite set of **players**.
- $S_i$ is the set of **pure strategies** available to player $i$, and $S = \prod_{i \in N} S_i$ is the joint strategy space.
- $u_i : S \to \mathbb{R}$ is the **payoff (utility) function** of player $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Extended-form games** add a temporal structure: a game tree with nodes representing decision points, edges representing actions, and information sets representing what each player knows when moving.

## 1.2 The Three Foundational Ingredients of Any Game

| Ingredient | What It Captures | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Players** | Who is making the decisions? | Two chess masters seated across the board |
| **Strategies** | What can each player *do*? | The set of legal moves on a chessboard |
| **Payoffs** | What does each player *want*? | The tournament points and prestige at stake |

## 1.3 Competitive Equilibrium — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Competitive (or Walrasian) Equilibrium** is a state of a market in which (i) every consumer maximizes utility given prices and wealth, and (ii) every firm maximizes profit given prices, such that **aggregate demand equals aggregate supply** for every good simultaneously.

- A price vector $p^* = (p_1^*, p_2^*, \dots, p_m^*)$ is a **competitive equilibrium** if:
  - **Household optimality:** Each household chooses its most-preferred affordable bundle.
  - **Firm optimality:** Each firm chooses its profit-maximizing production plan.
  - **Market clearing:** $\sum_i x_i(p^*, m_i) = \sum_j y_j(p^*) $ for every good $k$.

## 1.4 Rationality — The Heart of Strategic Thinking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rationality** means a player has well-defined preferences (represented by a utility function $u_i$) and, when facing a well-defined choice problem, always selects the action (or strategy) that **maximizes** his/her expected utility given his/her beliefs about what others will do.

Two levels of rationality must be distinguished:
- **Individual Rationality:** A player never knowingly chooses a strictly dominated option. Formally, $s_i$ is *individually rational* if there is no other strategy $\hat{s}_i$ such that $u_i(\hat{s}_i, s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$ for every $s_{-i}$.
- **Collective (Group) Rationality:** A group of players achieve a *Pareto-efficient* outcome — no player can be made better off without making at least one other player worse off.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Knowledge of Rationality (CKR):** A foundational assumption. "I am rational. I know that you are rational. I know that you know that I am rational…" and so on, ad infinitum. This infinite regress is the engine that drives **iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies (IESDS)**.

## 1.5 Real-World Analogy: The Beach vs. the Movies

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine two friends, **A and B**, deciding where to go on a Saturday. A prefers the **beach**, B prefers the **movies**. But they care more about *being together* than about the venue. Formally, B will sometimes go to the beach to be with A, and A will sometimes go to the movies to be with B. Equilibrium occurs when **neither wants to deviate** — a clear illustration of how strategic interdependence resolves into mutual best responses.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sequential Logic of Strategic Analysis

A complete game-theoretic analysis, in the canonical order taught at KTU, proceeds through the following steps:

- **Step 1 — Model the situation** as a game: identify $N$, $S_i$, $u_i$.
- **Step 2 — Determine the solution concept** that will be applied (dominant strategy, iterated dominance, Nash equilibrium, competitive equilibrium, etc.).
- **Step 3 — Predict behaviour** by finding the strategies/market prices that satisfy the chosen solution concept.
- **Step 4 — Verify rationality & efficiency** — is the prediction self-enforcing, and is it Pareto-efficient?

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why model?** Because without an explicit mathematical structure, the analyst cannot distinguish intuition from genuine strategic truth.
- **Why a solution concept?** Many "equilibria" exist; the concept selects the *credible* ones (where no player wishes to deviate unilaterally).
- **Why verify efficiency?** A Nash equilibrium need **not** be socially optimal (the classic Prisoner's Dilemma). The **price of anarchy** measures this gap.
- **Why check common knowledge?** Without CKR, the infinite-regress argument underpinning iterated dominance collapses.

## 2.3 Key Solution Concepts — Definitions

- **Strictly Dominant Strategy:** $s_i^*$ strictly dominates $s_i$ if $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$ for every $s_{-i} \in S_{-i}$.
- **(Pure-strategy) Nash Equilibrium:** A profile $s^* = (s_1^*, \dots, s_n^*)$ such that, for every player $i$:
$$u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \;\ge\; u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*) \quad \forall \, s_i \in S_i$$
- **Competitive (Walrasian) Equilibrium:** A price $p^* \in \mathbb{R}^m_+$ and an allocation $(x^*, y^*)$ such that
  - $(x_i^*)$ is a utility maximizer for household $i$ given $(p^*, m_i)$,
  - $(y_j^*)$ is a profit maximizer for firm $j$ given $p^*$,
  - $\sum_i x_i^* = \sum_j y_j^*$ (market-clearing for all goods, with free disposal of any excess).
- **Pareto Efficiency:** An allocation $z^*$ is Pareto-efficient if there is no feasible $z$ with $u_i(z) \ge u_i(z^*)$ for all $i$ and $u_k(z) > u_k(z^*)$ for some $k$.

## 2.4 The Two Fundamental Welfare Theorems (Why Equilibrium Matters)

- **First Welfare Theorem (FWT):** Under convexity, continuity, and completeness of markets, every competitive equilibrium allocation is **Pareto-efficient**.
- **Second Welfare Theorem (SWT):** Under the same regularity conditions, every Pareto-efficient allocation can be supported as a competitive equilibrium by an appropriate reallocation of initial endowments.

These two theorems form the bridge between the **individual rationality** of price-taking agents and the **collective rationality** of social optimality.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Boundary / Domain Conditions | Engineering / Economic Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic-form game | $G = (N, S_i, u_i)$ | Finite $N$, finite $S_i$ for standard results | Modelling auctions, network routing, spectrum allocation |
| Nash Equilibrium (NE) | $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \ge u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*) \; \forall i, \forall s_i$ | Pure strategy requires finite game; mixed requires compactness (Nash 1950) | Predicts stable behaviour in oligopolies, wireless contention |
| Competitive equilibrium | $\max u_i(x_i)$ s.t. $p \cdot x_i \le m_i$ and $\sum x_i = \sum y_j$ | Locally non-satiated preferences; convex production sets | Distributed resource allocation, market-clearing in smart grids |
| Strict dominance | $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) \; \forall s_{-i}$ | Strict inequality everywhere | Algorithm for IESDS — used in mechanism design simplification |
| Mixed strategy NE | $\sigma_i^*$ maximizes $\mathbb{E}[u_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i})]$ | Probability simplex $\Delta(S_i)$ | Randomised access protocols, ALOHA, blockchain mining |
| Best response | $BR_i(s_{-i}) = \arg\max_{s_i \in S_i} u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$ | Existence requires compact $S_i$ and continuous $u_i$ | Network design, security games |
| Price of Anarchy | $\text{PoA} = \frac{\text{Worst NE social cost}}{\text{Optimal social cost}}$ | Always $\text{PoA} \ge 1$ | Quantifies inefficiency in routing, congestion games |
| Welfare Theorems | FWT: CE $\Rightarrow$ PE; SWT: PE $\Rightarrow$ CE (with transfers) | Convex, continuous, complete markets | Justification of free-market decentralised allocation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** KTU examiners frequently award zero marks when a student writes "$\ge$" instead of "$>$" in a *strict* dominance statement, or when equilibrium conditions omit the "$ \forall s_i \in S_i$" quantifier. Memorize the strictness qualifier for dominance-based questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Example — The Cournot Duopoly (Foundational Derivation)

> **Background:** Two firms, **Firm 1** and **Firm 2**, produce a homogeneous good with **inverse demand** $P(Q) = a - bQ$ where $Q = q_1 + q_2$. Each firm has constant marginal cost $c$ (with $a > c$, $b > 0$). We will derive the Nash equilibrium and then contrast it with the competitive (price-taking) outcome.

### Step 1: Write each firm's profit function

Firm $i$'s revenue is $P(Q) \cdot q_i = (a - b(q_1 + q_2))q_i$. Total cost is $c \cdot q_i$. Therefore:

$$
\pi_i(q_i, q_j) \;=\; (a - b q_i - b q_j)\,q_i \;-\; c\,q_i
$$

Each row above and below must remain on its own line. Expanding the revenue term:

$$
\pi_i(q_i, q_j) \;=\; a q_i - b q_i^2 - b q_i q_j - c q_i
$$

### Step 2: Apply individual rationality (best-response derivation)

Firm $i$ chooses $q_i$ to maximize $\pi_i$, treating $q_j$ as given. The first-order condition is the partial derivative with respect to $q_i$ set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \pi_i}{\partial q_i} \;=\; a - 2b q_i - b q_j - c \;=\; 0
$$

Solving explicitly for $q_i$:

$$
2b q_i \;=\; a - c - b q_j \quad \Longrightarrow \quad q_i^{*}(q_j) \;=\; \frac{a - c}{2b} \;-\; \frac{q_j}{2}
$$

**This is the best-response function $BR_i(q_j)$** — a downward-sloping straight line, capturing the strategic substitutability of Cournot competition.

### Step 3: Find the symmetric Nash equilibrium

By symmetry, $q_1^* = q_2^* = q^*$. Substitute $q_j = q^*$ into the best response:

$$
q^{*} \;=\; \frac{a - c}{2b} \;-\; \frac{q^{*}}{2}
$$

Add $q^*/2$ to both sides and collect terms:

$$
q^{*} + \frac{q^{*}}{2} \;=\; \frac{a - c}{2b} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{3 q^{*}}{2} \;=\; \frac{a - c}{2b}
$$

Multiply both sides by $2/3$:

$$
q^{*} \;=\; \frac{a - c}{3b}
$$

Therefore each firm's equilibrium output is $q^N = \frac{a-c}{3b}$ and total output is:

$$
Q^{N} \;=\; \frac{2(a - c)}{3b}
$$

Equilibrium price is:

$$
P^{N} \;=\; a - b Q^{N} \;=\; a - b \cdot \frac{2(a-c)}{3b} \;=\; a - \frac{2(a-c)}{3} \;=\; \frac{a + 2c}{3}
$$

### Step 4: Derive the competitive (Walrasian) benchmark for comparison

Under perfect competition, $P = c$ (price equals marginal cost), so the quantity demanded solves $a - b Q^C = c$, giving:

$$
Q^{C} \;=\; \frac{a - c}{b}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the "Price of Anarchy" (PoA) and the welfare loss

Comparing total output:
$$
Q^{N} \;=\; \frac{2(a-c)}{3b} \;<\; \frac{a-c}{b} \;=\; Q^{C}
$$

So Cournot firms **under-produce** relative to the competitive outcome — the dead-weight loss triangle's base. The PoA in this symmetric duopoly for the total-welfare function $W(Q) = \int_0^Q (P(q) - c)\,dq$ is:

$$
\text{PoA} \;=\; \frac{W(Q^{C})}{W(Q^{N})} \;=\; \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}(a-c)\cdot\tfrac{a-c}{b}}{\tfrac{1}{2}(a-c)\cdot\tfrac{2(a-c)}{3b}} \;=\; \frac{3}{2}
$$

> **Result:** A 50% welfare loss relative to the competitive benchmark — a *quantitative* measure of inefficiency arising when individual rationality is not guided by prices.

## 3.2 Iterated Elimination of Strictly Dominated Strategies (IESDS) — Step-by-Step

> **Background:** In the $2\times 2$ game below, Row's payoffs are listed first.

$$
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
 & \text{Col: }L & \text{Col: }R \\
\hline
\text{Row: }U & (3,\,1) & (0,\,2) \\
\hline
\text{Row: }D & (1,\,0) & (2,\,3)
\end{array}
$$

**Step 1:** Inspect Column. Does Column have a strictly dominant strategy?
- If Col plays $L$: Row compares $3$ (from $U$) vs. $1$ (from $D$). $U$ is better.
- If Col plays $R$: Row compares $0$ (from $U$) vs. $2$ (from $D$). $D$ is better.
- *No* dominant strategy for Row. Symmetrically, none for Column.

**Step 2:** Eliminate any *strictly dominated* strategies by inspection. None present at round 1.

**Step 3:** Try **mixed** strategies. Let $p$ be the probability that Row plays $U$. Column's expected payoffs:
$$
EU_C(L) = p\cdot 1 + (1-p)\cdot 0 = p
$$
$$
EU_C(R) = p\cdot 2 + (1-p)\cdot 3 = 3 - p
$$
Setting $EU_C(L) = EU_C(R)$:
$$
p = 3 - p \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p = \tfrac{3}{2}
$$
Since $p > 1$, Row cannot make Column indifferent using pure mixes inside the simplex; rather, Row should mix to keep Column below indifference in $L$, but the **actual equilibrium** for this game is the **pure-strategy profile $(D,R)$** — each player's strategy is a best response to the other.

**Step 4:** Verify by checking the Nash condition at $(D,R)$:
- For Row: $u_1(D,R) = 2 \ge 0 = u_1(U,R)$. ✓
- For Col: $u_2(D,R) = 3 \ge 1 = u_2(D,L)$. ✓

**Conclusion:** $(D, R)$ is a **pure-strategy Nash equilibrium** and, after IESDS / best-response analysis, the unique self-enforcing outcome. The pair $(3/2, 1/2)$ would be the equilibrium *mix* only if payoffs were rebalanced — illustrating how **individual rationality** pins down the equilibrium.

## 3.3 The Existence Argument (Nash 1950) — Sketch

Consider a finite strategic-form game $G = \langle N, S_i, u_i \rangle$. Define the **best-response correspondence** for player $i$:
$$
BR_i(s_{-i}) \;=\; \arg\max_{s_i \in S_i}\; u_i(s_i, s_{-i})
$$

A **Nash equilibrium** is a *fixed point* of the composite correspondence:
$$
F(s) \;=\; \prod_{i \in N} BR_i(s_{-i}) \;\subseteq\; S
$$

By the **Kakutani fixed-point theorem** (Brouwer's generalization to set-valued maps), if:
- $S$ is **compact** and **convex** (a finite product of simplices qualifies),
- $F(s)$ is non-empty, **convex-valued**, and **upper hemi-continuous**,

then $\exists\, s^* \in S$ with $s^* \in F(s^*)$. In finite games, $BR_i$ is upper-hemi-continuous and non-empty, and since $S_i$ is a finite set, convexity holds trivially. Hence a **mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium** always exists in finite games.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Strategic-Form Game

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT LAYER Players N = 1 to n"] --> B["ACTION LAYER Pure Strategy Sets Si"]
    B --> C["INFORMATION LAYER Beliefs over S minus i"]
    C --> D["EVALUATION LAYER Utility Maps ui colon S to R"]
    D --> E["CONSTRAINT LAYER Individual Rationality Best Response"]
    E --> F{"SOLUTION CONCEPT"}
    F --> G["Strict Dominance Equilibrium"]
    F --> H["Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium"]
    F --> I["Competitive Walrasian Equilibrium"]
    F --> J["Subgame Perfect Equilibrium"]
    G --> K["OUTPUT PREDICTION Stable Play"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L["VERIFICATION Pareto Efficiency Welfare Theorems"]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — The Rationality Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step1 Preferences Complete Transitive"] --> S2["Step2 Beliefs Higher Order Beliefs"]
    S2 --> S3["Step3 CKR Common Knowledge of Rationality"]
    S3 --> S4["Step4 Best Response Maximize Expected Utility"]
    S4 --> S5["Step5 No Profitable Deviation Check"]
    S5 --> S6{"Equilibrium Reached"}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7["Nash or Competitive Equilibrium"]
    S6 -- No --> S8["Reassess Beliefs and Iterate"]
    S8 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Architecture — Competitive Equilibrium as a Distributed Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HH["Block A Households Maximize Utility Subject to Budget Constraint"] --> P["Block B Price Vector p asterisk"]
    P --> FF["Block C Firms Maximize Profit Given p asterisk"]
    FF --> P
    P --> MC{"Block D Market Clearing Total Demand equals Total Supply"}
    MC -- Yes --> EQ["Block E Equilibrium Allocation Pareto Efficient by FWT"]
    MC -- No --> P
```

## 4.4 Concept Map — Solution Concepts and Their Logical Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DS["Strictly Dominant Strategy"] --> IESDS["Iterated Elimination IESDS"]
    IESDS --> NASH["Pure Strategy Nash Equilibrium"]
    MAX["Maximin Strategy"] --> NASH
    NASH --> MIX["Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium"]
    MIX --> SUB["Subgame Perfect Equilibrium Extended Form"]
    CE["Competitive Equilibrium Walras"] --> PE["Pareto Efficiency"]
    PE --> SWT["Second Welfare Theorem Transfers"]
    NASH --> POA["Price of Anarchy Inefficiency Measure"]
```

## 4.5 Sequential Decision Tree — Strategy Elimination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Player i examines strategy profile s minus i"] --> R2["Compute ui for all si in Si"]
    R2 --> R3["Identify dominated strategies"]
    R3 --> R4{"Any strictly dominated"}
    R4 -- Yes --> R5["Remove dominated strategy"]
    R5 --> R6["Re evaluate remaining strategies"]
    R6 --> R3
    R4 -- No --> R7["Surviving strategies form reduced game"]
    R7 --> R8["Repeat until fixed point"]
    R8 --> R9["Outcome Rationalizable Strategies"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
> **Define the strategic (normal) form of a game and list its three primary components. Illustrate with a $2 \times 2$ example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A game in strategic (normal) form is the triplet $G = (N, S_i, u_i)$:
- **Players** $N$: the set of decision-makers, e.g., $N = \{1, 2\}$.
- **Strategies** $S_i$: the action set for player $i$, e.g., $S_1 = \{U, D\}$, $S_2 = \{L, R\}$.
- **Payoffs** $u_i$: a real-valued utility function over the joint strategy space, e.g., $u_1(U,L) = 3$, $u_1(D,R) = 2$.

**Example (Prisoner's Dilemma):**
$$
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
 & \text{Cooperate} & \text{Defect} \\
\hline
\text{Cooperate} & (3,3) & (0,4) \\
\hline
\text{Defect} & (4,0) & (1,1) \\
\end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explicitly identifies **players**, **strategies**, and **payoffs** — the three components examiners expect on a 3-mark question.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
> **Distinguish between individual rationality and collective rationality in the context of game theory. Provide a one-line example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Individual Rationality:** A player $i$ selects a strategy $s_i^*$ that maximizes *his/her* own payoff, $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}) \ge u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$, given others' actions. *Example:* In the Cournot duopoly, each firm chooses output that maximizes its own profit.
- **Collective (Group) Rationality:** A group of players reach an outcome that is **Pareto-efficient**, i.e., no reallocation can improve one player's welfare without harming another's. *Example:* The perfectly competitive market outcome, which is Pareto-efficient by the **First Welfare Theorem**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. [14 Marks – Sub-parts (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks]
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Define **Nash equilibrium** in pure strategies. Show, with the help of a $2 \times 2$ payoff matrix of your choice, the procedure for finding a Nash equilibrium by the **best-response method**.
>
> **(b)** State and explain the **two Fundamental Theorems of Welfare Economics**. Discuss the assumptions required for these theorems to hold.

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** A strategy profile $s^* = (s_1^*, \dots, s_n^*)$ is a **Nash equilibrium** if for every player $i \in N$ and for every $s_i \in S_i$:

$$u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \;\ge\; u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*)$$

**Example (5 marks):** Consider the following game:

$$
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
 & \text{Col: Left} & \text{Col: Right} \\
\hline
\text{Row: Up} & (4,3) & (1,1) \\
\hline
\text{Row: Down} & (2,2) & (3,4) \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 1 — Row's best response (2 marks):**
- If Column plays Left: $\max\{u_1(U,L)=4,\; u_1(D,L)=2\}$ → $BR_1(L) = U$.
- If Column plays Right: $\max\{u_1(U,R)=1,\; u_1(D,R)=3\}$ → $BR_1(R) = D$.

**Step 2 — Column's best response (2 marks):**
- If Row plays Up: $\max\{u_2(U,L)=3,\; u_2(U,R)=1\}$ → $BR_2(U) = L$.
- If Row plays Down: $\max\{u_2(D,L)=2,\; u_2(D,R)=4\}$ → $BR_2(D) = R$.

**Step 3 — Mutual best response (1 mark):** The unique Nash equilibrium is $(U, L)$ with payoffs $(4, 3)$.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**First Welfare Theorem (3 marks):** *Statement.* Under the assumptions listed below, **every competitive equilibrium allocation is Pareto-efficient**.
**Proof sketch:** If $(x^*, y^*, p^*)$ is a CE and not Pareto-efficient, then $\exists (x, y)$ feasible with $u_i(x_i) \ge u_i(x_i^*)$ for all $i$, strict for some $k$. Local non-satiation implies $p^* \cdot x_i \ge p^* \cdot x_i^*$ for all $i$ and $p^* \cdot x_k > p^* \cdot x_k^*$. Adding households' budgets: $\sum_i p^* \cdot x_i > \sum_i p^* \cdot x_i^*$. But profit maximisation gives $p^* \cdot y_j \le p^* \cdot y_j^*$, so $\sum_j p^* \cdot y_j \le \sum_j p^* \cdot y_j^*$, contradicting market clearing $\sum_i x_i^* = \sum_j y_j^*$ and $\sum_i x_i \le \sum_j y_j$ (feasibility). Therefore no such $(x, y)$ exists.

**Second Welfare Theorem (2 marks):** *Statement.* Under the same assumptions, **every Pareto-efficient allocation can be supported as a competitive equilibrium** by an appropriate redistribution of initial endowments.

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
- Markets are **complete** (a price for every good, including every future contingency).
- Preferences are **continuous, convex, and locally non-satiated**.
- Production sets are **convex** (constant or non-increasing returns).
- Perfect information and price-taking behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> - Writing "Pareto-efficient" without invoking the **price-taking** and **convexity** assumptions.
> - Confusing FWT with the result that *every* equilibrium is *socially optimal* (only the welfare-theorem claim is correct).
> - Forgetting to specify the role of **endowment transfers** in the SWT.

---

### Question B. [14 Marks – Sub-parts (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks]
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **common knowledge of rationality (CKR)**. Show how it leads to **iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies (IESDS)** using a three-player example.
>
> **(b)** Two firms compete in a **Bertrand duopoly** with homogeneous products, constant marginal cost $c$, and no capacity constraints. Derive the Nash equilibrium price. Comment on the **Bertrand paradox** and contrast it with the **Cournot** outcome.

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**CKR Definition (2 marks):** CKR is the joint statement:
- (i) Every player is rational (maximises expected utility).
- (ii) Every player knows that every other player is rational.
- (iii) Every player knows that (ii) holds, and so on **ad infinitum**.

**IESDS Procedure (3 marks):** Each round, every player uses the information that the others are rational to drop any strategy that is *strictly dominated* given the surviving strategies. The process repeats until no further strictly dominated strategies remain.

**Three-Player Illustration (2 marks):** Consider three players choosing between $\{A, B\}$. Payoffs satisfy: $A$ strictly dominates $B$ for player 1 *given any* profile of players 2 and 3 (and similarly for players 2 and 3 by symmetry of the dominance condition). Then at round 1, every player eliminates $B$, and the unique surviving profile is $(A, A, A)$. If the dominance were not strict, CKR alone would be insufficient — illustrating that **CKR + strictness** is the engine of IESDS.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Bertrand Setup (2 marks):** Two firms set prices $p_1, p_2$ for a homogeneous product with identical constant marginal cost $c$. Demand goes entirely to the lower-priced firm; if $p_1 = p_2$, demand is split.

**Best Response (3 marks):** Suppose firm 2 sets $p_2$. Firm 1's best response:
$$
p_1^*(p_2) \;=\; \begin{cases}
p_2 - \varepsilon & \text{if } p_2 > c \\
c & \text{if } p_2 \le c
\end{cases}
$$
A symmetric equilibrium is $p_1^* = p_2^* = c$.

**Bertrand Paradox (2 marks):** Even with only **two** firms and homogeneous products, price competition drives the equilibrium price down to marginal cost — the perfectly competitive outcome. This is the "paradox" of Bertrand: *duopoly* is enough to replicate *perfect competition* in prices, unlike in quantities (Cournot), where duopoly yields a markup. The contrast highlights how the **choice of strategic variable** (price vs. quantity) determines the equilibrium markup and the resulting welfare loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> - Forgetting to mention that the **no-capacity-constraint** assumption is crucial; with capacity limits, the equilibrium price exceeds marginal cost (Edgeworth cycle).
> - Confusing **Bertrand** (price competition) with **Cournot** (quantity competition) — examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this slip.
> - Stating the Bertrand equilibrium as $p^* = c$ without explicitly deriving the *undercutting incentive* $\varepsilon$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1)**

- **Game in normal form** $G = (N, S_i, u_i)$ — the atomic object of non-cooperative analysis.
- **Nash equilibrium** is a *fixed point* of the best-response correspondence; existence guaranteed in finite games by Kakutani's theorem (Nash 1950).
- **Strictly dominant strategy** $\Rightarrow$ Nash equilibrium. The converse is false (e.g., Battle of the Sexes).
- **Mixed-strategy NE** exists in every *finite* game; payoffs are computed as *expected* utilities over the probability simplex.
- **Common Knowledge of Rationality (CKR)** is the *engine* of iterated elimination; without it, IESDS is not self-enforcing.
- **Competitive (Walrasian) equilibrium** requires three conditions: household optimality, firm optimality, and market clearing.
- **First Welfare Theorem:** every competitive equilibrium is Pareto-efficient (under convexity, completeness, local non-satiation).
- **Second Welfare Theorem:** every Pareto-efficient allocation is a competitive equilibrium for some redistribution of endowments.
- **Cournot duopoly:** $q^N = \frac{a-c}{3b}$, $P^N = \frac{a+2c}{3}$, total output $\frac{2(a-c)}{3b}$, PoA $= 3/2$.
- **Bertrand duopoly:** $p^* = c$ (the **Bertrand paradox**), provided products are homogeneous and capacities are unconstrained.
- **Price of Anarchy (PoA)** quantifies the efficiency loss of strategic decentralisation; for the Cournot duopoly, PoA = $3/2$.
- **Pareto efficiency** is a *necessary* but not *sufficient* condition for social desirability (equity considerations are external to the Pareto criterion).
- **Always state strictness ($>$) vs. weak ($\ge$) dominance explicitly** — KTU examiners award full marks only when the inequality is precise.
- **Player best response is a function**, not a single point (unless the payoff function is strictly concave in $s_i$).
- **Distinguish "rational" from "smart"**: rationality means utility-maximising with given beliefs — beliefs themselves can be wrong (a *Bayesian* refinement addresses this).

---

> - **Topic:** Strategic Games - Dominance, Nash Equilibrium, Maxmin Strategies, Elimination of Dominated Strategies, Preservation of Pure Nash Equilibrium (PSNE), Matrix Games, Relation Between Maxmin and PSNE in Matrix Games, Mixed Strategies, Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium (MSNE), Finding MSNE, MSNE Characterization Theorem, Algorithm to Find MSNE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Strategic Games — The Foundation

A **Strategic Game** (also called a *Normal Form Game* or *Strategic Form Game*) is the most fundamental model in non-cooperative game theory. It is a complete description of the strategic interaction that specifies:
1. The set of **players** (decision-makers).
2. For each player, the set of **strategies** (actions available).
3. For each player, the **payoff function** (utility/preference over outcomes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A strategic game is a tuple $\Gamma = \langle N, (S_i)_{i \in N}, (u_i)_{i \in N} \rangle$, where:
> - $N = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ is a **finite** set of players.
> - $S_i$ is the **strategy set** of player $i$, with $S = S_1 \times S_2 \times \cdots \times S_n$ being the set of **strategy profiles**.
> - $u_i : S \to \mathbb{R}$ is the **payoff function** of player $i$, assigning a real number to every strategy profile.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Chess Board with Money"
Imagine two friends, Anu and Balu, deciding where to eat. Anu wants *Pizza*, Balu wants *Dosa*. They must choose *simultaneously* without calling each other. The "rules of the game" are: who is choosing, what are the options, and how happy is each person with every possible pair of choices. This *complete description* is a strategic game. The choices are the **strategies**, and the happiness scores are the **payoffs**.

## 1.2 Dominance — "Why Play a Losing Move?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iterated Strict Dominance:** A strategy $s_i$ **strictly dominates** $s_i'$ for player $i$ if for every strategy profile $s_{-i}$ of the opponents, $u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i', s_{-i})$. A **weakly dominated** strategy has $u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) \geq u_i(s_i', s_{-i})$ with strict inequality for at least one $s_{-i}$.

**Intuition:** If a strategy gives you *strictly worse* payoff than another strategy no matter what your opponent does, you would *never rationally* play it. This is the bedrock of *rationalizability*.

## 1.3 Nash Equilibrium (NE) — "The Stable Handshake"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nash Equilibrium:** A strategy profile $s^* = (s_1^*, s_2^*, \dots, s_n^*)$ is a **Nash Equilibrium** if for every player $i$ and every strategy $s_i \in S_i$:
> $$u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*)$$

**Intuition:** At a Nash Equilibrium, *no player has any incentive to unilaterally deviate*. Everyone is playing a *best response* to what the others are doing. Think of two bicycles cruising at the same speed on a parallel track — neither wants to swerve.

## 1.4 Maxmin Strategy — "Worst-Case Insurance"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maxmin Strategy:** A strategy $s_i^* \in S_i$ is a **maxmin strategy** for player $i$ if it maximizes the worst-case payoff:
> $$s_i^* \in \arg\max_{s_i \in S_i} \min_{s_{-i} \in S_{-i}} u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$$
> The corresponding value $v_i = \min_{s_{-i}} u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i})$ is the **security level** or **maxmin value**.

**Intuition:** A maxmin strategy is the "pessimist's choice" — assume your opponent will do the worst to you, then pick the action that makes that *worst case* as *good as possible*. Like carrying an umbrella *even if* the weather is sunny.

## 1.5 Mixed Strategies & MSNE — "The Power of Randomness"

A **Mixed Strategy** is a probability distribution over the pure strategies. Formally, a mixed strategy for player $i$ is $\sigma_i \in \Delta(S_i)$, where $\Delta(S_i)$ is the set of all probability distributions on $S_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium (MSNE):** A mixed strategy profile $\sigma^* = (\sigma_1^*, \dots, \sigma_n^*)$ is an MSNE if for every player $i$ and every mixed strategy $\sigma_i \in \Delta(S_i)$:
> $$u_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}^*)$$

**Intuition:** When no pure strategy profile is stable (e.g., matching pennies, penalty kicks), players must *randomize* to remain unpredictable. The MSNE is a randomization pattern where the opponent is *indifferent* among the pure strategies in the support, and hence cannot exploit the randomization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Best Response Correspondence

The **Best Response** of player $i$ to a strategy profile $s_{-i}$ of others is the set of strategies that maximize $i$'s payoff:

$$BR_i(s_{-i}) = \arg\max_{s_i \in S_i} u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$$

A strategy profile $s^*$ is a Nash Equilibrium if and only if $s_i^* \in BR_i(s_{-i}^*)$ for every player $i$.

## 2.2 Dominance — Layered Structure

- **Strict Dominance:** $s_i \succ s_i'$ means $u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i', s_{-i})$ for all $s_{-i}$.
- **Weak Dominance:** $s_i \succeq s_i'$ means $u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) \geq u_i(s_i', s_{-i})$ for all $s_{-i}$, with strict inequality for at least one $s_{-i}$.
- **Strict Dominance Solvability:** A game is solvable by iterated strict dominance if iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies leaves a single outcome.

## 2.3 Maxmin vs. Minmax in Two-Player Zero-Sum Games

For a two-player zero-sum game where $u_1 + u_2 = 0$:

$$\underline{v} = \max_{\sigma_1} \min_{\sigma_2} u_1(\sigma_1, \sigma_2) = \min_{\sigma_2} \max_{\sigma_1} u_1(\sigma_1, \sigma_2) = \bar{v}$$

This is the **Minimax Theorem** (von Neumann, 1928). The common value is the **value of the game**.

## 2.4 Expected Payoff in Mixed Strategies

For a two-player game with mixed strategies $\sigma_1 = (p, 1-p)$ and $\sigma_2 = (q, 1-q)$ on $2 \times 2$ pure strategy matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$ for player 1:

$$E[u_1(\sigma_1, \sigma_2)] = p \cdot q \cdot a + p(1-q) \cdot b + (1-p) q \cdot c + (1-p)(1-q) \cdot d$$

$$= pq(a - b - c + d) + p(b - d) + q(c - d) + d$$

## 2.5 Indifference (Equalizing) Principle for MSNE

In a $2 \times 2$ game with no PSNE, the MSNE for player 1 is found by setting the opponent indifferent across both of player 2's pure strategies:

$$p \cdot a + (1-p) \cdot c = p \cdot b + (1-p) \cdot d$$

Solving:

$$p^* = \frac{d - c}{(a - c) - (b - d)} = \frac{d - c}{a - b - c + d}$$

Similarly, by symmetry, the MSNE for player 2:

$$q^* = \frac{d - b}{(a - b) - (c - d)} = \frac{d - b}{a - b - c + d}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formulas for KTU Board Examinations**

| **Concept** | **Formula / Condition** | **Validity / Boundary** |
|-------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Nash Equilibrium | $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*)$ for all $i, s_i$ | For all finite strategic games |
| Strict Dominance | $u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i', s_{-i})$ for all $s_{-i}$ | Strict inequality everywhere |
| Weak Dominance | $u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) \geq u_i(s_i', s_{-i})$ for all $s_{-i}$ | At least one strict inequality |
| Maxmin Strategy | $\arg\max_{s_i} \min_{s_{-i}} u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$ | Always exists in finite games |
| Minmax Strategy | $\arg\min_{s_{-i}} \max_{s_i} u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$ | For two-player zero-sum |
| MSNE Expected Payoff | $\sum_{s_1, s_2} \sigma_1(s_1) \sigma_2(s_2) u_1(s_1, s_2)$ | Probabilities sum to **1** |
| Indifference Condition (P1) | $p a + (1-p) c = p b + (1-p) d$ | Valid when $0 \leq p^* \leq 1$ |
| MSNE $p^*$ formula | $p^* = (d - c) \mid (a - b - c + d)$ | Requires denominator $\neq 0$ |
| MSNE $q^*$ formula | $q^* = (d - b) \mid (a - b - c + d)$ | Requires denominator $\neq 0$ |
| Value of Zero-Sum Game | $\underline{v} = \bar{v}$ | Holds for any finite zero-sum game |
| Nash's Existence Theorem | Every finite game has a mixed-strategy NE | Continuous payoff function |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Telecommunications:** Power control in CDMA networks modeled as a non-cooperative game where base stations are players and transmit powers are strategies.
2. **Cybersecurity:** Attacker-defender games for resource allocation in intrusion detection systems.
3. **Smart Grid:** Demand-response management where consumers (players) randomize consumption to minimize peak load.
4. **Spectrum Auction Design:** FCC auctions use game theory to allocate radio spectrum efficiently among telecom companies.
5. **Autonomous Vehicles:** Merging and lane-changing decisions modeled as a matrix game with safety payoffs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Prisoner's Dilemma (PSNE via Dominance)

Consider the classical **Prisoner's Dilemma** payoff matrix (Player 1 rows, Player 2 columns):

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} (-1, -1) & (-9, 0) \\ (0, -9) & (-6, -6) \end{pmatrix}$$

where rows = $\{C, D\}$ (Cooperate, Defect) and columns = $\{C, D\}$.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: Identify the game structure.**
- Player 1's payoff matrix: $A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -9 \\ 0 & -6 \end{pmatrix}$
- Player 2's payoff matrix: $A_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ -9 & -6 \end{pmatrix}$

**Step 2: Check for strict dominance for Player 1.**

Compare $C$ vs $D$ for Player 1:
- If Player 2 plays $C$: $u_1(C, C) = -1$, $u_1(D, C) = 0$. So $D \succ C$ (since $0 > -1$).
- If Player 2 plays $D$: $u_1(C, D) = -9$, $u_1(D, D) = -6$. So $D \succ C$ (since $-6 > -9$).

**Conclusion:** $D$ strictly dominates $C$ for Player 1.

**Step 3: Check for strict dominance for Player 2.**

Compare $C$ vs $D$ for Player 2:
- If Player 1 plays $C$: $u_2(C, C) = -1$, $u_2(C, D) = 0$. So $D \succ C$ (since $0 > -1$).
- If Player 1 plays $D$: $u_2(D, C) = -9$, $u_2(D, D) = -6$. So $D \succ C$ (since $-6 > -9$).

**Conclusion:** $D$ strictly dominates $C$ for Player 2.

**Step 4: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS).**

After eliminating $C$ for both players, only $(D, D)$ remains with payoff $(-6, -6)$.

**Step 5: Nash Equilibrium.**

$(D, D)$ is the **unique Pure Strategy Nash Equilibrium (PSNE)** and also the outcome of IEDS.

**Step 6: Verify the PSNE condition.**

$$u_1(D, D) = -6 \geq u_1(C, D) = -9 \quad \checkmark$$
$$u_2(D, D) = -6 \geq u_2(D, C) = -9 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Matching Pennies (No PSNE, MSNE Exists)

**Matching Pennies:** Player 1 wants to match, Player 2 wants to mismatch.

$$A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad A_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: Check for PSNE.**

Check all four profiles:
- $(H, H)$: $u_1 = 1$, deviation to $T$ gives $u_1 = -1$. So $(H, H)$ is NE? Yes for P1. But $u_2(H,H) = -1$, deviation to $T$ gives $u_2 = 1$. So P2 wants to deviate. **Not NE.**
- $(H, T)$: $u_1 = -1$, deviation to $T$ gives $u_1 = 1$. P1 deviates. **Not NE.**
- $(T, H)$: $u_1 = -1$, deviation to $H$ gives $u_1 = 1$. P1 deviates. **Not NE.**
- $(T, T)$: $u_1 = 1$, P1 stays. $u_2 = 1$, deviation to $H$ gives $u_2 = -1$. P2 stays. **PSNE? No wait, $u_1(T,T) = 1$, deviating to $H$ gives $u_1 = -1$. P1 stays. $u_2(T,T) = 1$, deviating to $H$ gives $u_2 = -1$. P2 stays. So $(T, T)$ IS a PSNE.**

**Correction:** Actually in matching pennies, $T$ vs $T$ gives mismatch (because they differ), so player 2 (the mismatcher) wins. So $u_1(T,T) = -1$, $u_2(T,T) = 1$. Let me redo with corrected standard matching pennies matrix.

**Corrected Standard Matching Pennies:**

$$A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad A_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Here Player 1 wins if pennies match, Player 2 wins if they differ.

**Step 2: Re-check for PSNE.**

- $(H, H)$: $u_1 = 1$ (match), $u_2 = -1$. P1: deviating to $T$ gives $u_1 = -1$ (mismatch). P1 stays. P2: deviating to $T$ gives $u_2 = 1$ (mismatch). P2 deviates. **Not NE.**
- $(H, T)$: $u_1 = -1$, P1 deviates to $T$ for $u_1 = 1$. **Not NE.**
- $(T, H)$: $u_1 = -1$, P1 deviates to $H$ for $u_1 = 1$. **Not NE.**
- $(T, T)$: $u_1 = 1$ (match), $u_2 = -1$. P1 stays. P2 deviates to $H$ for $u_2 = 1$. **Not NE.**

**No PSNE exists.**

**Step 3: Set up indifference conditions for MSNE.**

Let Player 1 play $H$ with probability $p$, and Player 2 play $H$ with probability $q$.

Player 1's expected payoff from playing $H$:
$$E[u_1 \mid H] = q \cdot 1 + (1-q)(-1) = 2q - 1$$

Player 1's expected payoff from playing $T$:
$$E[u_1 \mid T] = q(-1) + (1-q)(1) = -2q + 1$$

For Player 1 to be willing to mix, set these equal:
$$2q - 1 = -2q + 1 \implies 4q = 2 \implies q^* = \frac{1}{2}$$

Player 2's expected payoff from playing $H$:
$$E[u_2 \mid H] = p(-1) + (1-p)(1) = -2p + 1$$

Player 2's expected payoff from playing $T$:
$$E[u_2 \mid T] = p(1) + (1-p)(-1) = 2p - 1$$

For Player 2 to be willing to mix, set these equal:
$$-2p + 1 = 2p - 1 \implies 4p = 2 \implies p^* = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4: Verify MSNE.**

$\sigma_1^* = (1/2, 1/2)$ and $\sigma_2^* = (1/2, 1/2)$.

**Expected payoff to Player 1 at MSNE:**
$$E[u_1] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot (-1) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot (-1) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1 = 0$$

The value of this zero-sum game is $v = 0$, and both maxmin and minmax equal $0$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Battle of the Sexes (PSNE + MSNE)

$$A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 2, 1 & 0, 0 \\ 0, 0 & 1, 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

Player 1 prefers Opera, Player 2 prefers Football, but both prefer being together.

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1: Find PSNE.**

- $(O, O)$: $u_1 = 2$ (best for P1), $u_2 = 1$ (best for P2, since $u_2(F,O) = 0$). **PSNE!**
- $(O, F)$: $u_1 = 0$ (P1 deviates to $F$ for $u_1 = 1$). **Not NE.**
- $(F, O)$: $u_1 = 0$ (P1 deviates to $O$ for $u_1 = 2$). **Not NE.**
- $(F, F)$: $u_1 = 1$ (best, since $u_1(O,F) = 0$), $u_2 = 2$ (best, since $u_2(F,O) = 0$). **PSNE!**

**Two PSNE: $(O, O)$ and $(F, F)$.**

**Step 2: Find MSNE.**

Let Player 1 play $O$ with probability $p$, Player 2 play $O$ with probability $q$.

Player 1's payoff from $O$: $2q + 0(1-q) = 2q$.
Player 1's payoff from $F$: $0q + 1(1-q) = 1-q$.

Indifference: $2q = 1 - q \implies 3q = 1 \implies q^* = 1/3$.

Player 2's payoff from $O$: $1(1-p) + 0 \cdot p = 1 - p$.
Player 2's payoff from $F$: $0(1-p) + 2p = 2p$.

Indifference: $1 - p = 2p \implies 3p = 1 \implies p^* = 1/3$.

**MSNE:** $\sigma_1^* = (1/3, 2/3)$, $\sigma_2^* = (1/3, 2/3)$.

**Expected payoffs at MSNE:**
$$E[u_1] = \frac{1}{3} \cdot 2 = \frac{2}{3}, \quad E[u_2] = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 1 = \frac{2}{3}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: MSNE Characterization Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MSNE Characterization Theorem (Indifference Principle):**
> In a $2 \times 2$ two-player game, a mixed strategy $\sigma_1^*$ is a best response to $\sigma_2^*$ if and only if:
> 1. Every pure strategy in the support of $\sigma_1^*$ is a best response to $\sigma_2^*$.
> 2. Every pure strategy NOT in the support of $\sigma_1^*$ yields a payoff $\leq$ that of pure strategies in the support.

### Derivation of the Indifference Condition

Consider a $2 \times 2$ game with Player 1's payoff matrix:
$$A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$$

Let Player 2 play column 1 with probability $q$ and column 2 with probability $1-q$.

**Player 1's expected payoff from playing row 1 (Top):**
$$U_T = a \cdot q + b \cdot (1-q) = b + q(a - b)$$

**Player 1's expected payoff from playing row 2 (Bottom):**
$$U_B = c \cdot q + d \cdot (1-q) = d + q(c - d)$$

For Player 1 to mix between Top and Bottom with positive probability, both must yield equal expected payoff (indifference condition):

$$b + q(a - b) = d + q(c - d)$$

$$q(a - b - c + d) = d - b$$

$$q^* = \frac{d - b}{a - b - c + d}$$

This holds provided $a - b - c + d \neq 0$. If $a - b - c + d = 0$, then either both pure strategies are always equally good (any mix is BR) or one strictly dominates (no mixing possible).

By symmetric reasoning for Player 2:
$$p^* = \frac{d - c}{a - b - c + d}$$

The probability is in $[0, 1]$ if and only if the signs of the numerator and denominator match.

## 3.5 Relation Between Maxmin and PSNE in Matrix Games

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** In any two-player finite strategic game, if a strategy profile $s^* = (s_1^*, s_2^*)$ is a PSNE, then:
> $$s_1^* \in \arg\max_{s_1} \min_{s_2} u_1(s_1, s_2)$$
> $$s_2^* \in \arg\min_{s_2} \max_{s_1} u_1(s_1, s_2)$$

### Proof Outline

Let $(s_1^*, s_2^*)$ be a PSNE. Then for all $s_1$:
$$u_1(s_1^*, s_2^*) \geq u_1(s_1, s_2^*)$$

Taking minimum over $s_2$:
$$\min_{s_2} u_1(s_1^*, s_2) \leq u_1(s_1^*, s_2^*)$$

Wait — this is the *opposite* direction. The correct relation is:

For any $s_1$, $s_2$:
$$u_1(s_1, s_2) \leq \max_{s_1'} u_1(s_1', s_2)$$

So at a PSNE $(s_1^*, s_2^*)$:
$$\min_{s_2} u_1(s_1^*, s_2) \leq u_1(s_1^*, s_2^*) \leq \max_{s_1} u_1(s_1, s_2^*)$$

This means: at a PSNE, the value of the maxmin equals the value of the minmax, and PSNE strategies are maxmin/minmax strategies. In a two-player zero-sum game with PSNE, **maxmin = minmax = value of game**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Converse is FALSE:** A maxmin strategy is *not necessarily* part of a PSNE. In the Battle of the Sexes, the maxmin strategy for Player 1 is $F$ (which gives worst-case payoff of 0), but PSNE exists at $(O, O)$ and $(F, F)$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    checkA -- No --> contA[Use Infinite Game Methods]

    checkB -- No --> checkC{2 Player Zero Sum?}
    checkC -- Yes --> zeroA[Use Minimax Theorem]
    checkC -- No --> checkD{2x2 Game?}
    checkD -- Yes --> msneA[Apply Indifference Principle]
    checkD -- No --> lpsolA[Use Linear Programming LCP]
    msneA --> msneverifyA[Verify 0 to 1 Probability]
    zeroA --> verifymixedA[Verify Mixed Strategy NE]
    psneA --> endnodeA[Output Equilibrium Strategies]
```

## 4.2 Best Response Correspondence and Equilibrium

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigma1[Sigma 1 Opponent Strategy] --> brfunc[BR2 Function]
    sigma2[Sigma 2 Player 2 Strategy] --> brfunc
    brfunc --> bestresp[Best Response Set BR2 Sigma Minus 2]
    bestresp --> necheck{Is it NE?}
    necheck -- Yes --> neout[Nash Equilibrium]
    necheck -- No --> neback[Deviation Exists]
    brfunc2[BR1 Function] --> bestresp2[Best Response Set BR1 Sigma Minus 1]
    bestresp2 --> necheck
    style sigma1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0
    style sigma2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
```

## 4.3 Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS) Process

```mermaid

    givengame[Original Game G] --> step1[Step 1 Identify Strictly Dominated Strategies]
    step1 --> step1out[Remove Dominated Rows and Columns]
    step1out --> step2check{Game Reduced?}
    step2check -- Yes --> step2[Step 2 Check New Dominance]
    step2check -- No --> step3check{Single Strategy Profile Left?}
    step3check -- Yes --> solved[Solved Game Unique Outcome]
    step3check -- No --> step2
    step2 --> step1out
    solved --> finalout[Solution by IEDS]
```

## 4.4 Algorithm to Find MSNE in 2x2 Games (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startAlgo[Input 2x2 Payoff Matrix A1 A2] --> chkpsne[Check All 4 Pure Strategy Profiles]
    chkpsne --> psnefound{PSNE Found?}
    psnefound -- Yes --> outputpsne[Output PSNE and Stop]
    psnefound -- No --> setupindiff[Set Up Indifference Equations for Both Players]
    setupindiff --> solveP1[Solve for q Star Player 2 Mix]
    setupindiff --> solveP2[Solve for p Star Player 1 Mix]
    solveP1 --> checkP1{0 less or equal q Star less or equal 1?}
    solveP2 --> checkP2{0 less or equal p Star less or equal 1?}
    checkP1 -- Yes --> valid1[Valid Mixed Probability]
    checkP1 -- No --> invalid1[Invalid No MSNE in this Region]
    checkP2 -- Yes --> valid2[Valid Mixed Probability]
    checkP2 -- No --> invalid2[Invalid No MSNE in this Region]
    valid1 --> finalcheck[Combine into MSNE Profile]
    valid2 --> finalcheck
    invalid1 --> finalcheck
    invalid2 --> finalcheck
    finalcheck --> outputmsne[Output MSNE with p Star q Star]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Strict Dominance and Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A strategy $s_i \in S_i$ **strictly dominates** $s_i' \in S_i$ for player $i$ if for every strategy profile $s_{-i} \in S_{-i}$ of the opponents:

$$u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i', s_{-i})$$

This means $s_i$ is *strictly better* than $s_i'$ regardless of what the opponents do.

**Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies (IEDS):** IEDS is the procedure of repeatedly removing strictly dominated strategies from the strategy sets, simplifying the game step by step until no more such strategies exist. The remaining strategies form the set of *rationalizable strategies*. A game is **dominant-strategy solvable** if IEDS yields a unique outcome.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** IEDS does not always yield a unique outcome (e.g., Battle of the Sexes has two PSNE, and IEDS does not eliminate either). However, IEDS is guaranteed to preserve all PSNE of the game.

### Question 2: State Nash's Existence Theorem
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Nash's Theorem (1950):** Every finite strategic-form game with a finite number of players, each having a finite set of strategies, possesses at least one **Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium (MSNE)**.

**Implications:**
1. The set of MSNE is *non-empty* for every finite game.
2. The set of MSNE is a *compact, convex, upper hemi-continuous* correspondence.
3. Every finite game has an MSNE, but a *pure* strategy NE may not exist (e.g., matching pennies).

**Necessary Conditions:** Finite $N$, finite $S_i$, and $u_i : S \to \mathbb{R}$ well-defined.

**Sufficient Conditions:** Brouwer's fixed point theorem guarantees existence when strategy sets are compact and convex (mixed strategies), and payoff functions are continuous.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A: Dominance, PSNE, and Iterated Elimination
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`** *(14 marks)*

Consider the following two-player game where Player 1 chooses rows $\{A, B, C\}$ and Player 2 chooses columns $\{X, Y, Z\}$:

|   | **X** | **Y** | **Z** |
|---|-------|-------|-------|
| **A** | 4, 3 | 1, 2 | 2, 5 |
| **B** | 2, 1 | 5, 4 | 0, 3 |
| **C** | 3, 6 | 2, 0 | 4, 2 |

#### Part (a): Find all Pure Strategy Nash Equilibria (PSNE). Verify using the best response condition. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the best responses for Player 1 (rows).

For each column, find the row that maximizes Player 1's payoff:
- Column $X$: $\max\{4, 2, 3\} = 4 \Rightarrow$ Row $A$.
- Column $Y$: $\max\{1, 5, 2\} = 5 \Rightarrow$ Row $B$.
- Column $Z$: $\max\{2, 0, 4\} = 4 \Rightarrow$ Row $C$.

**Step 2:** Compute the best responses for Player 2 (columns).

For each row, find the column that maximizes Player 2's payoff:
- Row $A$: $\max\{3, 2, 5\} = 5 \Rightarrow$ Column $Z$.
- Row $B$: $\max\{1, 4, 3\} = 4 \Rightarrow$ Column $Y$.
- Row $C$: $\max\{6, 0, 2\} = 6 \Rightarrow$ Column $X$.

**Step 3:** PSNE is where both best responses coincide.

Check each profile:
- $(A, X)$: P1's BR to $X$ is $A$ ✓. P2's BR to $A$ is $Z$, not $X$ ✗. **Not PSNE.**
- $(A, Y)$: P1's BR to $Y$ is $B$, not $A$ ✗. **Not PSNE.**
- $(A, Z)$: P1's BR to $Z$ is $C$, not $A$ ✗. **Not PSNE.**
- $(B, X)$: P1's BR to $X$ is $A$, not $B$ ✗. **Not PSNE.**
- $(B, Y)$: P1's BR to $Y$ is $B$ ✓. P2's BR to $B$ is $Y$ ✓. **PSNE!** Payoff = $(5, 4)$.
- $(B, Z)$: P1's BR to $Z$ is $C$, not $B$ ✗. **Not PSNE.**
- $(C, X)$: P1's BR to $X$ is $A$, not $C$ ✗. **Not PSNE.**
- $(C, Y)$: P1's BR to $Y$ is $B$, not $C$ ✗. **Not PSNE.**
- $(C, Z)$: P1's BR to $Z$ is $C$ ✓. P2's BR to $C$ is $X$, not $Z$ ✗. **Not PSNE.**

**Unique PSNE: $(B, Y)$ with payoff $(5, 4)$.**

#### Part (b): Apply Iterated Elimination of Strictly Dominated Strategies (IESDS). Does IESDS preserve the PSNE? *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Check for strictly dominated strategies for Player 1.

Compare rows $A$, $B$, $C$:
- $A$ vs $B$: $(4 \text{ vs } 2), (1 \text{ vs } 5), (2 \text{ vs } 0)$. Inconsistent — neither dominates.
- $A$ vs $C$: $(4 \text{ vs } 3), (1 \text{ vs } 2), (2 \text{ vs } 4)$. Inconsistent.
- $B$ vs $C$: $(2 \text{ vs } 3), (5 \text{ vs } 2), (0 \text{ vs } 4)$. Inconsistent.

**No strictly dominated row for Player 1.**

**Step 2:** Check for strictly dominated strategies for Player 2.

Compare columns $X$, $Y$, $Z$:
- $X$ vs $Y$: $(3 \text{ vs } 2), (1 \text{ vs } 4), (6 \text{ vs } 0)$. Inconsistent.
- $X$ vs $Z$: $(3 \text{ vs } 5), (1 \text{ vs } 3), (6 \text{ vs } 2)$. Inconsistent.
- $Y$ vs $Z$: $(2 \text{ vs } 5), (4 \text{ vs } 3), (0 \text{ vs } 2)$. Inconsistent.

**No strictly dominated column for Player 2.**

**Step 3:** Check for *weakly* dominated strategies.

For Player 1, compare $A$ and $C$:
- $(4 \text{ vs } 3), (1 \text{ vs } 2), (2 \text{ vs } 4)$. Here $C \geq A$ in two columns with strict inequality at $Y$ and $Z$? No — at $Y$, $1 < 2$, so $A < C$ in column $Y$. And at $X$, $A > C$. So neither weakly dominates.

For Player 1, is $B$ weakly dominated? Compare $B$ and $A$: $(2<4), (5>1), (0<2)$. No. Compare $B$ and $C$: $(2<3), (5>2), (0<4)$. No.

**Conclusion:** No strictly or weakly dominated strategies exist for either player. IESDS does not eliminate any strategy.

**Step 4:** Preservation of PSNE.

Since IESDS eliminated nothing, the original game is preserved, and the PSNE $(B, Y)$ remains in the reduced game. The PSNE of the original game is a PSNE of the reduced game — **this is the preservation property of IESDS.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies **always preserves all PSNE**, though it may not eliminate all non-equilibrium strategies.

---

### Question B: Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Supplementary Exam 2023]`** *(14 marks)*

Consider the following two-player zero-sum game where Player 1 is the row player and Player 2 is the column player:

|   | **L** | **R** |
|---|-------|-------|
| **U** | 3 | -1 |
| **D** | -2 | 4 |

Player 1's payoff matrix: $A_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.

#### Part (a): Show that this game has no Pure Strategy Nash Equilibrium. Hence find the value of the game and the Maxmin strategy for each player. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Check for PSNE.

A PSNE requires both players' best responses to coincide. Let's check:

- $(U, L)$: $u_1 = 3$. P1's alternative is $D$ with $u_1 = -2$. So P1 stays. P2's alternative to $L$ is $R$, giving P2 payoff $-3$ (zero-sum). P2 stays. **PSNE!**

Wait, let me recompute. In a zero-sum game, $u_2 = -u_1$. So:
- $(U, L)$: $u_1 = 3, u_2 = -3$. P1 alternative $D$: $u_1 = -2$. P1 stays. P2 alternative $R$: $u_2 = -(-1) = 1$. P2 deviates. **Not PSNE.**
- $(U, R)$: $u_1 = -1$. P1 alternative $D$: $u_1 = 4$. P1 deviates. **Not PSNE.**
- $(D, L)$: $u_1 = -2$. P1 alternative $U$: $u_1 = 3$. P1 deviates. **Not PSNE.**
- $(D, R)$: $u_1 = 4, u_2 = -4$. P1 alternative $U$: $u_1 = -1$. P1 stays. P2 alternative $L$: $u_2 = 2$. P2 deviates. **Not PSNE.**

**No PSNE exists.**

**Step 2:** Maxmin strategy for Player 1 (Pessimistic).

Player 1 considers each pure strategy and computes the worst-case payoff:
- If P1 plays $U$: $\min\{3, -1\} = -1$.
- If P1 plays $D$: $\min\{-2, 4\} = -2$.

Maxmin: $\max\{-1, -2\} = -1$. So maxmin value for P1 is $-1$, achieved by playing $U$ (pure maxmin).

**Maxmin strategy for P1:** $U$ (with probability 1).

**Step 3:** Minmax strategy for Player 2.

Player 2 considers each pure strategy and computes the worst-case (to P1) payoff, then minimizes:
- If P2 plays $L$: $\max\{3, -2\} = 3$.
- If P2 plays $R$: $\max\{-1, 4\} = 4$.

Minmax: $\min\{3, 4\} = 3$. So minmax value for P2 is $3$, achieved by playing $L$ (pure minmax).

**Minmax strategy for P2:** $L$ (with probability 1).

**Step 4:** Verify via the formula.

In a zero-sum game, maxmin $\leq$ minmax. Here $-1 < 3$, so the strict inequality holds for pure strategies, confirming no PSNE and no pure equilibrium value.

#### Part (b): Find the Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium (MSNE) of this game. Verify using the indifference condition. *(7 marks)*

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Set up probabilities.

Let Player 1 play $U$ with probability $p$ and $D$ with probability $1-p$.
Let Player 2 play $L$ with probability $q$ and $R$ with probability $1-q$.

**Step 2:** Indifference condition for Player 1.

Player 1's expected payoff from $U$:
$$E[u_1 \mid U] = 3q + (-1)(1-q) = 4q - 1$$

Player 1's expected payoff from $D$:
$$E[u_1 \mid D] = -2q + 4(1-q) = -6q + 4$$

For mixing, set equal:
$$4q - 1 = -6q + 4$$
$$10q = 5$$
$$q^* = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** Indifference condition for Player 2.

Player 2's expected payoff (which is $-u_1$) from $L$:
$$E[u_2 \mid L] = -(3p + (-2)(1-p)) = -5p + 2$$

Player 2's expected payoff from $R$:
$$E[u_2 \mid R] = -(-p + 4(1-p)) = 5p - 4$$

For mixing, set equal:
$$-5p + 2 = 5p - 4$$
$$10p = 6$$
$$p^* = 0.6$$

**Step 4:** Verify MSNE.

MSNE: $\sigma_1^* = (0.6, 0.4)$ and $\sigma_2^* = (0.5, 0.5)$.

**Expected payoff to Player 1:**
$$E[u_1] = 4(0.5) - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1$$
$$E[u_1] = -6(0.5) + 4 = -3 + 4 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Value of the game:** $v = 1$.

**Verification of best responses:**

- P1's $U$ payoff: $4(0.5) - 1 = 1$.
- P1's $D$ payoff: $-6(0.5) + 4 = 1$.
- P1 is indifferent between $U$ and $D$ ✓.

- P2's $L$ payoff: $-(5(0.6) - 2) = -(3 - 2) = -1$.
- P2's $R$ payoff: $-(-5(0.6) + 4) = -(-3 + 4) = -1$.
- P2 is indifferent between $L$ and $R$ ✓.

**Conclusion:** The unique MSNE is $\sigma_1^* = (0.6, 0.4), \sigma_2^* = (0.5, 0.5)$ with value $v = 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors in zero-sum games:** Remember $u_1 + u_2 = 0$, so Player 2's payoff is the *negative* of Player 1's. A single sign slip costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Indifference equation set-up:** The equation must be between payoffs of the *same player* across *different strategies*, not across different players. Mixing up players is a fatal error worth **3 marks** loss.
> 3. **Probability bounds:** Always verify $0 \leq p^* \leq 1$ and $0 \leq q^* \leq 1$. If the value lies outside this range, there is no MSNE of the assumed form (full support).
> 4. **Forgetting to check PSNE first:** Many students jump directly to mixed strategies without first checking whether a PSNE exists. This loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing maxmin with minmax:** Maxmin is the player's *own* worst-case guarantee; minmax is the opponent's *attack*. Do not interchange them in a zero-sum game — it is worth **2 marks**.
> 6. **Skipping verification:** KTU examiners award up to **2 marks** specifically for verifying the indifference condition and probability bounds.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ A **strategic game** is fully specified by players, strategy sets, and payoff functions.
- ☐ **Strict dominance**: a strategy strictly worse than another for *all* opponent actions.
- ☐ **Weak dominance**: a strategy weakly worse for all, strictly worse for at least one.
- ☐ **Nash Equilibrium**: no player has incentive to unilaterally deviate; equivalent to all players playing mutual best responses.
- ☐ **PSNE**: a Nash Equilibrium in pure strategies — may or may not exist.
- ☐ **MSNE**: a Nash Equilibrium in mixed strategies — *always* exists for finite games (Nash, 1950).

### Key Theorems
- ☐ **Nash's Existence Theorem (1950)**: Every finite game has at least one MSNE.
- ☐ **Minimax Theorem (von Neumann, 1928)**: In finite two-player zero-sum games, maxmin = minmax = value of game.
- ☐ **MSNE Indifference Principle**: In a mixed NE, each player's support consists of pure strategies that yield *equal* expected payoff.
- ☐ **IESDS Preservation**: Iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies preserves all PSNE.

### Critical Formulas
- ☐ Nash condition: $u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*)$.
- ☐ Maxmin: $\arg\max_{s_i} \min_{s_{-i}} u_i(s_i, s_{-i})$.
- ☐ MSNE probabilities in $2 \times 2$: $p^* = (d - c) / (a - b - c + d)$, $q^* = (d - b) / (a - b - c + d)$.
- ☐ Expected payoff: $E[u] = \sum_{s_1, s_2} \sigma_1(s_1) \sigma_2(s_2) u(s_1, s_2)$.

### Common Pitfalls
- ☐ PSNE existence is **not guaranteed** (counterexample: Matching Pennies).
- ☐ Maxmin strategy is **not always a PSNE** (counterexample: Battle of the Sexes).
- ☐ Strict dominance implies rational elimination; weak dominance is more subtle.
- ☐ The MSNE indifference condition is *necessary* for full-support mixing, but *not* for partial-support mixing.

### Algorithm Steps to Find MSNE
1. ☐ Check all $m \times n$ profiles for PSNE first.
2. ☐ If no PSNE in a $2 \times 2$ game, set up indifference conditions.
3. ☐ Solve the linear equations for the opponent's mixing probabilities.
4. ☐ Verify $0 \leq p^* \leq 1$ and $0 \leq q^* \leq 1$.
5. ☐ Compute the expected payoff (value of game for zero-sum).
6. ☐ Confirm both players are indifferent within the support.

### Real-World Applications
- ☐ **Auctions**: Spectrum auctions, ad auctions.
- ☐ **Cybersecurity**: Attacker-defender resource allocation.
- ☐ **Telecom**: Power control in CDMA, OFDMA.
- ☐ **Smart Grid**: Demand-side management.
- ☐ **Economics**: Oligopoly pricing, Cournot/Bertrand competition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> 1. Always draw a 2x2 matrix clearly and label rows/columns.
> 2. For PSNE questions, show the best-response correspondence explicitly with arrows or markings.
> 3. For MSNE, write down the indifference equations *symbolically first*, then substitute numerical values.
> 4. Always verify at the end: check probabilities, check best response, compute the value.
> 5. In CO3-evaluated questions, the **step-by-step derivation** is worth more than the final answer; the *process* carries at least 60% of the marks.

topics.title: Correlated Equilibrium (CE) - Computing CE, Extensive Form Games, Subgame Perfection, Limitations of Subgame Perfect Nash Equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST756 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Correlated Equilibrium (CE) - Computing CE, Extensive Form Games, Subgame Perfection, Limitations of Subgame Perfect Nash Equilibrium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Correlated Equilibrium (CE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Aumann, 1974):**  
> A *correlated equilibrium* of a strategic-form game $\Gamma = (N, (A_i), (u_i))$ is a joint probability distribution $p$ over the set of action profiles $A = \prod_{i \in N} A_i$ such that, for every player $i \in N$ and for every action $a'_i \in A_i$ that player $i$ might be tempted to deviate to:
> $$\sum_{a_{-i} \in A_{-i}} p(a_i, a_{-i}) \cdot [u_i(a_i, a_{-i}) - u_i(a'_i, a_{-i})] \geq 0 \quad \forall a_i \in A_i$$

In essence, CE generalizes Nash Equilibrium by allowing **correlated randomization** through a public/private signal mechanism. A trusted "mediator" or random device draws an action profile $a = (a_1, \ldots, a_n)$ from distribution $p$ and *privately recommends* $a_i$ to each player $i$. The equilibrium condition states: *no player has an incentive to deviate from the recommended action*, assuming all other players also follow their recommendations.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Light

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine two cars approaching an uncontrolled intersection. A **traffic light** is essentially a *correlated equilibrium device*. The light randomly recommends "Go" (Green) or "Stop" (Red) to each driver. If you see Green, you know the other driver is receiving the *complementary* signal (Red) under a proper correlation scheme. You would not want to deviate (run a red) because that would create a costly collision. The traffic light thus coordinates behavior **without drivers explicitly communicating** — a perfect illustration of correlation replacing strategic deliberation.

**Why it is a "Generalization" of Nash:** Every mixed-strategy Nash equilibrium is a correlated equilibrium, but the converse is **not true**. The set of correlated equilibria is a **convex polytope** that strictly contains the Nash equilibrium set. This is the geometric reason why CE often yields **higher social welfare** than Nash (as demonstrated in the famous *Battle of the Sexes* and *Penalty Kick* examples).

---

## 1.2 Extensive Form Games (EFG)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**  
> An *extensive form game* is a tuple $\Gamma = \langle N, H, P, (u_i), \mathcal{I} \rangle$ where:
> - $N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ is the finite set of players.
> - $H$ is the set of non-terminal *histories* (sequences of actions) including the empty history $\emptyset$ (root node).
> - $P : H \setminus Z \to N \cup \{c\}$ is the *player function* assigning the decision-maker at each non-terminal history (where $Z$ is the set of terminal histories and $c$ denotes "chance"/Nature).
> - $(u_i)_{i \in N}$ is the profile of *von Neumann–Morgenstern utility functions* $u_i : Z \to \mathbb{R}$.
> - $\mathcal{I}_i$ is the *information partition* of player $i$, partitioning the histories where $P(h) = i$ into information sets $I \in \mathcal{I}_i$. All histories in one information set have the *same available actions* $A(I)$.

**Perfect Information** vs **Imperfect Information:**
- *Perfect information*: every information set is a singleton — the player knows exactly which node she is at.
- *Imperfect information*: at least one information set contains multiple histories — the player cannot distinguish between them (modeled by a **dashed oval** in tree diagrams).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Chess Game

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A chess game is a *perfect information* extensive form game. At every turn, the player knows the *exact* state of the board (which pieces remain, positions, etc.). The history is the sequence of all prior moves. In contrast, a *poker* game is an *imperfect information* EFG: you do not know the opponent's hidden cards, so the relevant histories are bundled into information sets representing "states you cannot distinguish."

---

## 1.3 Subgame Perfect Nash Equilibrium (SPNE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Selten, 1965):**  
> A strategy profile $s^* = (s_1^*, \ldots, s_n^*)$ is a *subgame perfect Nash equilibrium* of an extensive form game $\Gamma$ if it constitutes a Nash equilibrium in *every subgame* of $\Gamma$. Formally:
> $$\forall G' \subseteq \Gamma, \quad s^*_{\vert G'} \text{ is a Nash equilibrium of } G'$$
> where a *subgame* $G'$ is a subtree rooted at some non-terminal history $h \in H$ such that $h$ and all its successors belong entirely to a single information set for every player.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Credible Plan

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** SPNE eliminates "cheap talk." Consider a parent promising a child, "If you misbehave, I will cancel the birthday party AND we will never go to Disneyland." The Nash equilibrium of the one-shot game might include this threat, but in any *subgame* reached after misbehavior, the rational parent would still take the child to Disneyland (since doing so is in the parent's own interest). SPNE, computed by **backward induction**, recognizes that the *second* part of the threat is **non-credible** and must be excluded.

---

## 1.4 Limitations of Subgame Perfect Nash Equilibrium

> [!WARNING]
> **Why SPNE Alone is Insufficient:**
> 1. **The Empty Threat Problem:** In some sequential games, SPNE can be supported by *non-credible threats* because no proper subgame exists at the threat point (e.g., the threat is made at the last move where no future subgame remains to penalize deviation).
> 2. **Limited Credibility for Punishments in Repeated Games:** SPNE does not model the *future cost* of reputation in repeated interactions — the **Folk Theorem** shows many non-cooperative equilibria are SPNE in the infinitely repeated game.
> 3. **Insensitivity to Off-Path Beliefs:** SPNE places no restrictions on beliefs at information sets that are *not reached* in equilibrium, allowing multiple equilibria differing only in off-path behavior.
> 4. **Weakness with Incomplete Information (Bayesian Games):** SPNE does not extend naturally to Bayesian extensive games; one must use *perfect Bayesian equilibrium*.
> 5. **Multiple SPNE:** SPNE can be non-unique when the game has multiple subgames with multiple Nash equilibria — selection problem.
> 6. **Inability to Capture Correlation:** Like NE, SPNE does not incorporate *correlated* strategies arising from external signals or mediators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Conceptual Architecture of Correlated Equilibrium

The CE concept rests on **four pillars** that must be clearly understood:

1. **Mediator / Correlation Device:** An external random device samples a profile $a = (a_1, \ldots, a_n)$ from a publicly known distribution $p$ and privately recommends $a_i$ to player $i$.
2. **Bayesian Updating:** Upon receiving $a_i$, player $i$ forms a posterior belief $\mu_i(\cdot \mid a_i)$ over the *joint* profile using Bayes' rule:
   $$\mu_i(a_{-i} \mid a_i) = \frac{p(a_i, a_{-i})}{\sum_{a'_{-i}} p(a_i, a'_{-i})}$$
   (defined for all $a_i$ such that the marginal $p_i(a_i) > 0$).
3. **Incentive Compatibility (IC):** For every recommended $a_i$ in the support and every deviation $a'_i$:
   $$\sum_{a_{-i}} \mu_i(a_{-i} \mid a_i) \cdot u_i(a_i, a_{-i}) \;\geq\; \sum_{a_{-i}} \mu_i(a_{-i} \mid a_i) \cdot u_i(a'_i, a_{-i})$$
4. **No Communication Beyond Recommendation:** Players cannot exchange signals *other than* the recommendation from the device.

### Why Each Step Matters

- Step 1 introduces a *trusted randomness source* that allows the equilibrium concept to break the independence assumption of mixed NE.
- Step 2 is the **mathematical mechanism** of correlation — beliefs become *contingent* on observed signals.
- Step 3 is the **equilibrium condition** — players must *want* to follow recommendations.
- Step 4 ensures the concept is *decentralized* and respects information asymmetry.

---

## 2.2 Computing Correlated Equilibrium — The LP Formulation

Computing a CE is a **Linear Programming** problem. The standard form is:

> **Decision Variables:** $p(a) \geq 0$ for every action profile $a \in A$.

> **Constraints:**
> 1. **Probability normalization:**
> $$\sum_{a \in A} p(a) = 1$$
> 2. **Incentive compatibility (one per player-action pair):**
> $$\sum_{a_{-i}} p(a_i, a_{-i}) \cdot [u_i(a_i, a_{-i}) - u_i(a'_i, a_{-i})] \geq 0 \quad \forall i, \forall a_i, \forall a'_i$$
> 3. **Non-negativity:** $p(a) \geq 0$.

> **Objective:** Maximize any linear social-welfare function such as
> $$\max \; W = \sum_{a \in A} p(a) \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i(a)$$

This LP is **always feasible** (pure NE corresponds to a corner point) and **always bounded** for finite games. The set of feasible solutions is a convex polytope, so extreme points can be found via the Simplex method or interior-point methods.

### Why Use LP for CE?
- **Polynomial-time solvability:** unlike computing Nash equilibria (PPAD-complete), CE is computable in polynomial time in the size of the game.
- **Captures all correlated equilibria** (not just extreme points).
- Enables welfare optimization across the entire CE polytope.

---

## 2.3 Subgame Perfection via Backward Induction

The standard algorithm:

1. Identify all subgames of $\Gamma$ (subtrees rooted at singletons of information sets).
2. Start from the *deepest* subgames — solve for the Nash equilibrium(s) at terminal subgames.
3. Replace each subgame with a single node whose payoff is the equilibrium payoff.
4. Iterate upward — at each level, treat the lower subgames as atomic choices with computed payoffs.
5. At the root, the resulting strategies form an SPNE (or one of possibly many).

### Limitations Deep-Dive

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SPNE Fails to Refine Empty Threats:** Consider a two-stage game: Player 1 moves first, Player 2 observes and responds. Suppose Player 1's move $L$ leads to a subgame where Player 2 prefers to play $a$, and move $R$ leads to a subgame where Player 2 is indifferent but *threatens* $b$ (a punishment). SPNE will include the threat $b$ because in the subgame following $R$, both $a$ and $b$ are Nash equilibria, so the threat is *credible in that subgame*. But strategically, the threat may be empty if Player 2 would not have actually wanted to commit to $b$ at the outset — yet SPNE selects it because the subgame is trivially Nash. **This is the empty-threat problem.**

**The Subgame Perfect $\epsilon$-Equilibrium** and **Sequential Equilibrium** (Kreps & Wilson, 1982) are refinements designed to address some of these issues, particularly off-path beliefs in games of imperfect information.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| CE incentive constraint | $\sum_{a_{-i}} p(a_i, a_{-i}) \cdot [u_i(a_i, a_{-i}) - u_i(a'_i, a_{-i})] \geq 0$ | No profitable deviation given private signal $a_i$ |
| Posterior belief (Bayes) | $\mu_i(a_{-i} \mid a_i) = \dfrac{p(a_i, a_{-i})}{\sum_{a'_{-i}} p(a_i, a'_{-i})}$ | Conditional probability of opponents' actions |
| EFG definition | $\Gamma = \langle N, H, P, u_i, \mathcal{I} \rangle$ | Tuple of 5 components |
| SPNE condition | $s^*_{\vert G'} \text{ is NE } \forall G' \subseteq \Gamma$ | Nash in every subgame |
| Subgame definition | Subtree at history $h$ contained in a single info-set | Well-defined reduced game |
| Backward induction | Start from leaves, propagate optimal payoffs upward | Computes SPNE in finite perfect-info games |
| Welfare objective (LP) | $\max \sum_a p(a) \cdot \sum_i u_i(a)$ | Sum of utilities weighted by CE probabilities |
| Folk Theorem (limitation) | Any feasible payoff above min-max is SPNE in $G^{\infty}$ | Repeated games have many SPNE — selection problem |
| Sequential equilibrium | $(s^*, \mu)$ consistent + sequential rationality | Refines SPNE for imperfect info |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** CE appears in *decentralized network routing* (e.g., Google's WAN traffic engineering), *auction design* (FCC spectrum auctions), *multi-agent robotics* coordination, *cryptographic protocols* (MPC), and *smart grid* demand-response — anywhere correlated randomness from environmental or mediated sources improves over independent randomized control.
