> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Affine Maximizers, Single Object Allocation, Myerson's Lemma, Optimal Mechanism Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Affine Maximizers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Affine Maximizer** is an allocation rule $x : [0, \bar{v}]^n \rightarrow \Delta(O)$ (a probability distribution over outcomes) that can be expressed as the solution to a linear program of the form:
>
> $$x^*(v) = \arg\max_{o \in O} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot v_i \cdot x_i(o)$$
>
> where $w_i > 0$ are fixed non-negative weights assigned to each agent, and $x_i(o)$ denotes the contribution of agent $i$ to outcome $o$.

In simpler terms, an **affine maximizer** treats the reported bids of agents as if they were weighted by some constants, and then simply picks the outcome that maximizes the *weighted sum* of the bids. The "affine" part comes from the linear (affine) combination of the values, and "maximizer" describes the optimization performed over the outcome space.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The University Admissions Office**
> Imagine a university admissions office. The office wants to allocate one of two programs (CS or EE) to three students, each with a personal preference score (the "value" they assign to each program). The admissions office may not give equal importance to every student — perhaps the office multiplies each student's score by a *weight* (e.g., Alice's preferences count 1.5x, Bob's 1.0x, Carol's 0.8x) — and then assigns the program that maximizes the *total weighted score*. This rule, where the choice is determined by a fixed weighted linear combination of the reports, is exactly an **affine maximizer**.

The crucial insight is that **affine maximizers are the most general class of allocation rules that can be implemented in dominant strategies** when combined with an appropriate payment rule. This is the deep connection to **Myerson's Lemma** (Section 1.3 below).

---

## 1.2 Single Object Allocation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Single Object Allocation Problem** is a canonical single-parameter mechanism design environment in which a single indivisible item is to be allocated to one of $n$ self-interested agents (or to no one). Each agent $i$ has a private valuation $v_i \in [0, \bar{v}_i]$ drawn independently from a known prior distribution $F_i$ with density $f_i$. The designer seeks a mechanism $(x, p)$ — an allocation rule $x : [0,\bar{v}]^n \to \{0, 1, \ldots, n\}$ and a payment rule $p : [0,\bar{v}]^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ — that maximizes either **social welfare** (efficiency) or **expected revenue**.

Here, $x_i(v) = 1$ means the item is allocated to agent $i$, and $x_i(v) = 0$ means it is not. By feasibility, exactly one agent (or none) receives the item, so:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i(v) \leq 1 \quad \text{for all } v \in [0,\bar{v}]^n$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Selling a Vintage Painting at Auction**
> Picture an art auction house auctioning a single rare painting. Three collectors — Arti, Bavin, and Ciya — each privately value the painting differently (Arti might love it and value it at ₹10,000, Bavin at ₹7,000, and Ciya at ₹3,000). Each collector submits a sealed bid. The auction house wants to design a *mechanism* — a set of rules for who wins and who pays what — that incentivizes truthful bidding while maximizing either total social welfare (giving the painting to the person who values it most) or total revenue (extracting maximum payment). This is precisely the **Single Object Allocation Problem**.

The single object allocation setting is the **workhorse example** of single-parameter mechanism design. It is mathematically tractable, economically important, and provides the foundation for more complex problems like multi-object and combinatorial auctions.

---

## 1.3 Myerson's Lemma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Myerson's Lemma** (1981) is a fundamental characterization theorem for single-parameter environments. It states that a social choice function $x$ is implementable in dominant strategies (assuming the payment of agent $i$ depends only on her own bid $b_i$, and assuming $p_i(0) = 0$) **if and only if** the allocation rule $x_i$ is **monotone non-decreasing** in agent $i$'s bid. Moreover, when $x_i$ is monotone, the payment rule is **uniquely determined** (up to a constant) by the formula:
>
> $$p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_{0}^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) \, dz$$
>
> The constant is fixed by the **participation constraint** $p_i(0) = 0$, which is the standard *individual rationality* assumption.

This is one of the most important results in all of mechanism design. It completely characterizes implementable allocation rules and pins down the unique payment rule that incentivizes truth-telling.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Toll Booth Pricing**
> Imagine a highway with a single toll booth. The toll operator knows the historical distribution of cars' "willingness to pay" (their private values). Myerson's Lemma tells the operator two things: (1) The decision rule for *who gets through first* (the allocation) must be **monotonic** — if a particular car reports a higher value, it should never be moved *back* in the queue. (2) Once the queue-order rule is fixed, the *exact toll charge* is mathematically determined, leaving no flexibility. The toll operator cannot arbitrarily overcharge; the price is the **unique** price that makes truth-telling a best response.

The payment formula $p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_{0}^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$ has a beautiful geometric interpretation: it is precisely the **area to the left of the allocation curve** in the $(z, x_i)$ plane, scaled appropriately. This is the *critical payment* — the smallest payment that keeps the agent from wanting to misreport a lower value.

---

## 1.4 Optimal Mechanism Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Optimal Mechanism Design** is the problem of finding the mechanism $(x^*, p^*)$ that maximizes the designer's expected objective — typically **expected revenue** or **expected social welfare** — subject to the **incentive compatibility** (IC) and **individual rationality** (IR) constraints. For single-parameter environments, Myerson's Lemma reduces the IC constraints to **monotonicity** of the allocation rule, transforming an infinite-dimensional mechanism design problem into a tractable optimization over monotone allocation rules.

The standard formulation is:
$$\max_{x \text{ monotone}} \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i(v)\right]$$

subject to feasibility ($\sum_i x_i(v) \leq 1$) and IR ($p_i(0) = 0$).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Smartphone Manufacturer's Pricing Strategy**
> A smartphone company wants to set prices for a new phone to maximize total profit. They know (from market research) the distribution of willingness-to-pay among different customer segments: students, professionals, and premium buyers. The company faces a fundamental trade-off: setting a high uniform price earns lots from premium buyers but excludes students; setting a low price includes everyone but earns little from premium buyers. The **optimal mechanism** in this context is essentially a *second-degree price discrimination* scheme — offering different "packages" (e.g., base model vs. pro model) so that each customer *self-selects* into the package that is designed for their segment. The company's revenue-maximizing problem is mathematically equivalent to the **Optimal Mechanism Design** problem.

The solution to this problem is given by the **Virtual Valuation** approach (covered in Section 2.3), which generalizes the Myerson (1981) optimal auction and the standard monopoly pricing problem.

---

## 1.5 Summary of the Module's Logical Flow

The four topics form a beautiful logical chain:

1. **Single Object Allocation** defines the *environment* (the "what").
2. **Affine Maximizers** identify a particularly important *class* of implementable rules.
3. **Myerson's Lemma** provides the *characterization theorem* (the "how" of incentive compatibility).
4. **Optimal Mechanism Design** applies these tools to find the *best* mechanism (the "why it all matters").

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Affine Maximizers — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Formal Setup

Consider $n$ agents and a finite set of outcomes $O = \{o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_m\}$. Each agent $i$ has a private valuation function $v_i : O \to \mathbb{R}_+$. An allocation is a probability distribution over outcomes, represented as $x \in \Delta(O)$ where $x = (x(o_1), \ldots, x(o_m))$.

An **affine maximizer** has the form:
$$x(v) = \arg\max_{o \in O} \left\{\alpha_o + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot v_i \cdot x_i(o)\right\}$$

where:

- $\alpha_o \in \mathbb{R}$ is a *constant offset* associated with outcome $o$ (independent of reports).
- $w_i > 0$ is a *weight* associated with agent $i$ (independent of outcomes).
- $v_i$ is agent $i$'s reported value.

### 2.1.2 Why Are Affine Maximizers Important?

The deep result (due to Rochet, 1987) is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rochet's Theorem (1987):** A social choice function $f$ is implementable in dominant strategies (i.e., truthfully maximizable by some payment rule) **if and only if** $f$ can be represented as the choice from an affine maximizer.

In other words, **affine maximizers are exactly the set of implementable social choice functions** in environments with quasi-linear utilities. The weights $w_i$ and the offsets $\alpha_o$ jointly determine the structure of the implementation.

### 2.1.3 Special Case: Weighted Allocation

When all weights are equal ($w_i = 1$ for all $i$) and offsets are zero ($\alpha_o = 0$), the affine maximizer reduces to a **welfare maximizer**:
$$x(v) = \arg\max_{o \in O} \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot x_i(o)$$

This is the allocation rule that maximizes **social welfare** (the sum of all agents' values). By the **First Welfare Theorem**, this is the efficient outcome in a quasi-linear environment.

### 2.1.4 Examples in the Single Object Setting

For the single object allocation, the outcomes are $O = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ where outcome $i$ means "allocate to agent $i$" and outcome $0$ means "allocate to no one." The allocation rule $x_i(v) = 1$ if $i$ wins and $0$ otherwise.

An affine maximizer in this setting has the form:
$$i^*(v) = \arg\max_{i \in \{0,1,\ldots,n\}} \left\{\alpha_i + w_i \cdot v_i\right\}$$

with $\alpha_0 = w_0 = 0$ (so outcome 0 is the "no allocation" baseline).

---

## 2.2 Myerson's Lemma — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 The Monotonicity Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monotonicity (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An allocation rule $x_i : [0, \bar{v}_i] \to [0, 1]$ is **monotone non-decreasing** in agent $i$'s value if, for every $v_{-i}$ fixed, the function $z \mapsto x_i(z, v_{-i})$ is non-decreasing in $z$.

In the single object setting, monotonicity takes a particularly clean form: if agent $i$ wins the object at value $z$, she must also win the object at every value $z' \geq z$ (holding all other bids fixed). Equivalently, agent $i$'s "winning set" is an **upward-closed interval** in her bid space.

### 2.2.2 The Payment Formula — Geometric Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The payment $p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$ has a clean geometric meaning.
>
> - The integral $\int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$ is the *area under* the allocation curve $x_i$ from $0$ to $v_i$.
> - The term $v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i})$ is the *area of the rectangle* from $0$ to $v_i$ at height $x_i(v_i, v_{-i})$.
> - Their difference is therefore the *area between the curve and its bounding rectangle* — this is the **critical payment**.

For the single object setting where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is a step function, the formula simplifies:
- Let $z_i^*(v_{-i})$ be the smallest value at which agent $i$ wins the object (the **critical value**).
- Then $\int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz = (v_i - z_i^*) \cdot \mathbb{1}[v_i \geq z_i^*]$.
- The payment is $p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = z_i^*(v_{-i}) \cdot \mathbb{1}[v_i \geq z_i^*]$.

This is a beautiful result: **the payment equals the critical value at which the agent starts winning**, regardless of how high the actual bid is. The winner always pays exactly $z_i^*$ (the second-highest critical value, in a second-price-like mechanism).

### 2.2.3 The Payment Identity (Revenue Equivalence)

A direct corollary is the **Payment Identity**:
$$p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = \int_0^{v_i} z \cdot \frac{\partial x_i(z, v_{-i})}{\partial z} dz$$

obtained via integration by parts. This shows that the payment is determined entirely by the *derivative* (the "marginal allocation rate") of $x_i$.

### 2.2.4 Sufficiency of Monotonicity (Proof Sketch)

The proof of Myerson's Lemma has two directions:

**Necessity ($\Rightarrow$):** Suppose $(x, p)$ is IC. Consider agent $i$ with true value $v_i$ who could misreport as if her value were $v_i'$ (a "low-type deviation"). The IC constraint requires:
$$v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) \geq v_i \cdot x_i(v_i', v_{-i}) - p_i(v_i', v_{-i})$$

By the analogous inequality with $v_i$ and $v_i'$ swapped (swapping roles of "true" and "reported"), we can show that $x_i(v_i', v_{-i}) \leq x_i(v_i, v_{-i})$ whenever $v_i' \leq v_i$. This is monotonicity.

**Sufficiency ($\Leftarrow$):** Define the payment by the Myerson formula with $p_i(0) = 0$. Then for any misreport $v_i'$ with $v_i' < v_i$ (WLOG):
$$u_i(v_i) - u_i(v_i') = (v_i - v_i') \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_{v_i'}^{v_i} [x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - x_i(z, v_{-i})] dz \geq 0$$

by monotonicity of $x_i$. The case $v_i' > v_i$ is symmetric. So the mechanism is IC.

---

## 2.3 Optimal Mechanism Design — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 The Revenue Objective

Using Myerson's Lemma, the expected revenue from agent $i$ can be written as:
$$\mathbb{E}[p_i(v)] = \mathbb{E}\left[v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz\right]$$

Switching the order of integration and using $\int_0^{\bar{v}_i} \int_z^{\bar{v}_i} dv_i \, x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz = \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} (1 - F_i(z)) \cdot x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$ (with appropriate density weighting), we obtain:
$$\mathbb{E}[p_i(v)] = \mathbb{E}\left[\int_0^{\bar{v}_i} \phi_i(z) \cdot x_i(z, v_{-i}) \, dz\right]$$

where $\phi_i(z)$ is the **virtual valuation** of agent $i$ at value $z$.

### 2.3.2 Virtual Valuations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtual Valuation (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> For a distribution $F_i$ with density $f_i > 0$, the **virtual valuation** of agent $i$ at value $z$ is:
>
> $$\phi_i(z) = z - \frac{1 - F_i(z)}{f_i(z)}$$
>
> Equivalently, $\phi_i(z) = z - \frac{F_i(z)}{f_i(z)}$ in some textbooks depending on convention (care must be taken with the integration-by-parts sign).

The virtual valuation $\phi_i(z)$ represents the **marginal contribution of agent $i$ to expected revenue** when her allocation increases infinitesimally at value $z$.

### 2.3.3 The Regularity Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regularity (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A distribution $F$ is **regular** if the virtual valuation $\phi(z) = z - (1 - F(z))/f(z)$ is **strictly increasing** in $z$.

Regularity is a *monotonicity* property of the virtual valuation, and it is the key technical condition that ensures the optimal mechanism is implementable without "ironing."

### 2.3.4 The Optimal Mechanism (Single Object Case)

> [!NOTE]
> **Myerson's Optimal Auction (1981):**
> Assume values are i.i.d. from a regular distribution $F$. The revenue-maximizing mechanism for selling a single object is:
>
> 1. Compute each agent's virtual valuation $\phi_i(v_i)$.
> 2. Allocate the object to the agent with the **highest non-negative** virtual valuation. If all virtual valuations are negative, allocate to no one.
> 3. Charge the winner her **critical value** (the value at which her virtual valuation equals the second-highest virtual valuation).

When all agents are i.i.d. and the distribution is regular, this is exactly the **second-price auction with a reserve price**, where the reserve price $r^*$ is defined by $\phi(r^*) = 0$, i.e., $r^* = \frac{1 - F(r^*)}{f(r^*)}$.

---

| 3 | Monotonicity (Necessary) | $z \mapsto x_i(z, v_{-i})$ non-decreasing | For all $v_{-i}$ |

| 12 | Expected Revenue | $\mathbb{E}[R] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_i \phi_i(v_i) x_i(v)\right]$ | Regular distributions |
| 13 | Ironing | Replace non-monotone $\phi$ by convex hull | For irregular $F$ |

| 15 | Ironed Virtual Value | $\bar{\phi}_i(z) = $ slope of convex hull of $\phi_i$ | $\bar{\phi}_i$ is monotone by construction |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Myerson's Payment Formula

### 3.1.1 Setup

Assume agent $i$ has true value $v_i$ and reports $\hat{v}_i$. The utility to agent $i$ from report $\hat{v}_i$ when the others report $v_{-i}$ is:
$$u_i(\hat{v}_i \mid v_i) = v_i \cdot x_i(\hat{v}_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(\hat{v}_i, v_{-i})$$

### 3.1.2 Incentive Compatibility Conditions

**IC (Truth-Telling):** For all $v_i$ and all $\hat{v}_i$:
$$v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) \geq v_i \cdot x_i(\hat{v}_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(\hat{v}_i, v_{-i})$$

**IR (Individual Rationality):** $u_i(0 \mid v_i) \geq 0$, simplified to $p_i(0, v_{-i}) = 0$ (assuming $x_i(0, v_{-i}) = 0$, i.e., no allocation at zero value).

### 3.1.3 Envelope Theorem Application

Treating the utility function $U_i(\hat{v}_i) = v_i x_i(\hat{v}_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(\hat{v}_i, v_{-i})$ as a function of the *report* $\hat{v}_i$ (but the *true* $v_i$ appears as a coefficient), the envelope theorem tells us:

$$\frac{d U_i(\hat{v}_i)}{d \hat{v}_i}\bigg|_{\hat{v}_i = v_i} = x_i(v_i, v_{-i})$$

This is because at the truth-telling point, increasing the report by $\epsilon$ changes utility by approximately $v_i \cdot \partial x_i / \partial \hat{v}_i - \partial p_i / \partial \hat{v}_i$, but the IC constraint at the optimum forces the "informational rent" to be exactly $x_i$ (the standard envelope logic).

More rigorously, differentiate the IC condition with respect to $\hat{v}_i$ evaluated at $\hat{v}_i = v_i$:
$$\frac{d U_i}{d \hat{v}_i}\bigg|_{\hat{v}_i = v_i} = x_i(v_i, v_{-i})$$

### 3.1.4 Integration

Integrating from $0$ to $v_i$ (and using $U_i(0) = 0$ from $p_i(0) = 0$):
$$U_i(v_i) = \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for the Payment

Since $U_i(v_i) = v_i x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(v_i, v_{-i})$, we have:
$$v_i x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$$

Solving for $p_i$:
$$p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$$

This is the **Myerson Payment Formula** (Q.E.D.).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Revenue Objective in Terms of Virtual Valuations

### 3.2.1 Starting Point

From Myerson's formula, the expected payment from agent $i$ is:
$$\mathbb{E}[p_i(v)] = \mathbb{E}_{v_{-i}}\left[\mathbb{E}_{v_i}\left[v_i x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz \;\middle|\; v_{-i}\right]\right]$$

### 3.2.2 Inner Expectation — Step 1

Fix $v_{-i}$ and consider the function $g_i(z) = x_i(z, v_{-i})$. The inner expectation becomes:
$$E_{v_i}[v_i g_i(v_i)] - E_{v_i}\left[\int_0^{v_i} g_i(z) dz\right]$$

### 3.2.3 Inner Expectation — Step 2: Switch Order of Integration

For the second term, switch the order of integration:
$$\int_0^{\bar{v}_i} \int_z^{\bar{v}_i} g_i(z) f_i(v_i) \, dv_i \, dz = \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} g_i(z) \left[\int_z^{\bar{v}_i} f_i(v_i) dv_i\right] dz = \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} g_i(z) (1 - F_i(z)) dz$$

### 3.2.4 Inner Expectation — Step 3: Combine

So:
$$E_{v_i}[v_i g_i(v_i)] - \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} g_i(z) (1 - F_i(z)) dz = \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} z \cdot g_i(z) f_i(z) dz - \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} g_i(z) (1 - F_i(z)) dz$$

Combining:
$$= \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} g_i(z) \left[z f_i(z) - (1 - F_i(z))\right] dz = \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} g_i(z) \left[z - \frac{1 - F_i(z)}{f_i(z)}\right] f_i(z) dz$$

### 3.2.5 Recognizing the Virtual Valuation

Define $\phi_i(z) = z - (1 - F_i(z))/f_i(z)$. Then:
$$E_{v_i}[p_i \mid v_{-i}] = \int_0^{\bar{v}_i} \phi_i(z) \cdot x_i(z, v_{-i}) \cdot f_i(z) dz = E_{v_i}\left[\phi_i(v_i) \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i})\right]$$

### 3.2.6 Full Expected Revenue

Taking expectation over $v_{-i}$ as well:
$$\mathbb{E}[R] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \phi_i(v_i) \cdot x_i(v)\right]$$

**This is the central result of Myerson's optimal mechanism theory (Q.E.D.).**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Uniform Distribution, $n = 2$ Agents

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Two agents, single object, values $v_1, v_2 \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 1]$ independently. Find the revenue-maximizing mechanism.

### 3.3.1 Compute the Virtual Valuation

For $F(z) = z$ and $f(z) = 1$ on $[0, 1]$:
$$\phi(z) = z - \frac{1 - z}{1} = 2z - 1$$

So $\phi$ is increasing (regular), and $\phi(0) = -1$, $\phi(1) = 1$. The reserve price is $\phi(r^*) = 0 \Rightarrow r^* = 1/2$.

### 3.3.2 Optimal Mechanism

- If both $v_1 < 1/2$ and $v_2 < 1/2$: do not sell (both virtual valuations negative).
- Otherwise, sell to the agent with the higher $v_i$ (equivalently, higher $\phi(v_i) = 2v_i - 1$).
- Charge the winner a price of $1/2$ (the reserve price) if the loser also had $v \geq 1/2$, OR charge the loser's value if the loser had $v < 1/2$.

More precisely: the payment is $\max\{r^*, \text{other agent's value}\}$ — a second-price auction with reserve $1/2$.

### 3.3.3 Compute Expected Revenue

$$E[R] = 2 \cdot \int_{1/2}^{1} \int_{1/2}^{v_1} v_2 \cdot 1 \, dv_2 \, dv_1 + 2 \cdot \int_{1/2}^{1} \int_{0}^{1/2} (1/2) \cdot 1 \, dv_2 \, dv_1$$

Wait, we need to be careful with the symmetry. Let me redo this. The revenue comes from two scenarios:

**Scenario A:** Both values are $\geq 1/2$. Probability = $(1/2)^2 = 1/4$. The winner pays the loser's value.

**Scenario B:** One value $\geq 1/2$, the other $< 1/2$. Probability = $2 \cdot (1/2)(1/2) = 1/2$. The winner pays $1/2$.

**Scenario C:** Both values $< 1/2$. Probability = $1/4$. No sale.

Revenue in Scenario A: By symmetry, $E[v_{(2)} \mid v_1, v_2 \geq 1/2]$.
$$E[v_{(2)} \mid v_1, v_2 \geq 1/2] = \int_{1/2}^{1} \int_{1/2}^{v_1} v_2 \cdot \frac{1}{(1/2)^2} dv_2 dv_1 = 4 \int_{1/2}^{1} \frac{v_1^2 - 1/4}{2} dv_1 = 2 \int_{1/2}^{1} (v_1^2 - 1/4) dv_1$$

$$= 2 \left[\frac{v_1^3}{3} - \frac{v_1}{4}\right]_{1/2}^{1} = 2 \left[\left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{4}\right) - \left(\frac{1}{24} - \frac{1}{8}\right)\right] = 2 \left[\frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{12}\right] = \frac{1}{3}$$

So expected revenue from Scenario A: $(1/4) \cdot (1/3) = 1/12$.

Revenue in Scenario B: Each event gives $1/2$ with probability $1/2$ per event, so $(1/2) \cdot (1/2) = 1/4$.

Total expected revenue: $1/12 + 1/4 = 1/12 + 3/12 = 4/12 = 1/3$.

### 3.3.4 Verification via Virtual Surplus

We can verify using the virtual surplus formula:
$$E[R] = E[\phi_1(v_1) x_1 + \phi_2(v_2) x_2] = E[(2v_1 - 1) x_1 + (2v_2 - 1) x_2]$$

For our mechanism, $x_1 + x_2 = 1$ if $\max(v_1, v_2) \geq 1/2$ and the highest-valued agent gets the item, $0$ otherwise. Computing this confirms $E[R] = 1/3$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Exponential Distribution, $n$ Agents

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** $n$ agents, $v_i \sim \text{Exponential}(\mu)$ i.i.d., find optimal auction.

### 3.4.1 Virtual Valuation

For $F(z) = 1 - e^{-\mu z}$ and $f(z) = \mu e^{-\mu z}$:
$$\phi(z) = z - \frac{e^{-\mu z}}{\mu e^{-\mu z}} = z - \frac{1}{\mu}$$

The exponential distribution is **regular** (since $\phi$ is strictly increasing in $z$). The reserve price is $r^* = 1/\mu$.

### 3.4.2 Optimal Mechanism

- Allocate to the agent with the highest $v_i$ if $\max v_i \geq 1/\mu$; otherwise, do not sell.
- Charge the winner $\max\{1/\mu, \text{second highest value}\}$ — a second-price auction with reserve $1/\mu$.

This is the classical **Myerson (1981) result** for exponential distributions.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Mechanisms

| **Mechanism** | **Allocation Rule** | **Payment Rule** | **Best For** | **Limitations** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Vickrey (Second-Price) | Highest $v_i$ wins | Pay second-highest | Welfare maximization | Low revenue, no reserve |
| First-Price Sealed-Bid | Highest $v_i$ wins | Pay own bid | Revenue (with equilibrium) | Complex Bayesian analysis |
| Optimal (Myerson) | Highest $\phi_i \geq 0$ wins | Pay critical value | Revenue maximization | Requires distributional knowledge |
| Dutch Auction | Decreasing price, first taker wins | Pay current price | Speed, simple rules | Not strategy-proof |
| English Auction | Ascending price, last bidder wins | Pay final price | Intuitive, common value | Susceptible to signaling |

---

## 3.6 The Ironing Procedure (Sketch)

> [!NOTE]
> When the virtual valuation $\phi_i$ is **not monotone** (i.e., the distribution is **irregular**), the optimal mechanism requires *ironing*:
>
> 1. Plot the graph $\{(z, \phi_i(z))\}$ over the support of $F_i$.
> 2. Find the **convex hull** of the upper region of this graph.
> 3. Replace $\phi_i$ by the slope of the convex hull — call this the **ironed virtual valuation** $\bar{\phi}_i$.
> 4. $\bar{\phi}_i$ is now monotone non-decreasing by construction.
> 5. The optimal mechanism allocates to maximize $\sum_i \bar{\phi}_i(v_i) x_i(v)$.

The ironed virtual valuation satisfies $\bar{\phi}_i(z) \geq \phi_i(z)$ for all $z$ in the "ironed" region, which is why the optimal mechanism is *less* likely to allocate to agents with intermediate values — those are precisely the values where ironing "lifts" the virtual surplus.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**
> *State Myerson's Lemma for a single-parameter environment. Clearly define monotonicity and write the payment formula.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Myerson's Lemma (KTU 2024 Board Pattern Answer):**
>
> **Statement:** Consider a single-parameter environment with $n$ agents, each with private value $v_i \in [0, \bar{v}_i]$, and a quasi-linear utility setting. A social choice function $x$ is implementable in dominant strategies if and only if the allocation rule $x_i$ is **monotone non-decreasing** in agent $i$'s reported value. Furthermore, the payment rule is **uniquely determined** (up to an additive constant) by the formula:
>
> $$p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) \, dz$$
>
> The constant is fixed by the individual rationality condition $p_i(0, v_{-i}) = 0$.
>
> **Monotonicity (definition):** $x_i$ is monotone if, for any fixed $v_{-i}$, the function $z \mapsto x_i(z, v_{-i})$ is non-decreasing in $z$. (1 mark for monotonicity definition, 2 marks for full statement + formula.)

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 marks]**
> *Define an affine maximizer. State Rochet's theorem characterizing implementable social choice functions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Affine Maximizer (KTU 2024 Board Pattern Answer):**
>
> **Definition:** An **affine maximizer** is an allocation rule of the form $x(v) = \arg\max_{o \in O}\{\alpha_o + \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i v_i x_i(o)\}$, where $\alpha_o$ are outcome-specific constants and $w_i > 0$ are agent-specific weights.
>
> **Rochet's Theorem (1987):** A social choice function in a quasi-linear environment is implementable in dominant strategies **if and only if** it can be represented as the choice from an affine maximizer. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the theorem statement.)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, typically with sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. **Internal choice** means the student picks *one* of two alternatives. We provide both alternatives below with full worked solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks]**
> **(a)** *Consider a single object allocation with $n = 2$ agents whose values are drawn i.i.d. from Uniform$[0, 1]$. Compute the virtual valuation and derive the optimal revenue-maximizing mechanism. Clearly state the reserve price.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *For a single object auction with agent values i.i.d. from Exponential$(\mu)$ distribution, find the optimal auction, the reserve price, and the expected revenue in the large-$n$ limit.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Virtual Valuation (2 marks):**

For $F(z) = z$ and $f(z) = 1$ on $[0, 1]$:
$$\phi(z) = z - \frac{1 - F(z)}{f(z)} = z - \frac{1 - z}{1} = 2z - 1$$

**Step 2 — Reserve Price (1 mark):**

The reserve price $r^*$ satisfies $\phi(r^*) = 0$:
$$2r^* - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow r^* = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Optimal Allocation (2 marks):**

The object is sold to the agent with the **highest non-negative virtual valuation** $\phi_i(v_i) = 2v_i - 1$. Since $\phi$ is increasing and i.i.d., this is equivalent to selling to the agent with the highest $v_i$ if $\max(v_1, v_2) \geq 1/2$.

**Step 4 — Optimal Payment (1 mark):**

The winner pays her **critical value** $z^*$, which equals:
$$p_i = \max\left\{r^*, \text{other agent's value}\right\}$$

This is a **second-price auction with reserve price $1/2$**.

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark):**

The mechanism is implementable by Myerson's Lemma because $\phi(z) = 2z - 1$ is **monotone increasing** (regular distribution).

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Virtual Valuation (2 marks):**

For $F(z) = 1 - e^{-\mu z}$ and $f(z) = \mu e^{-\mu z}$:
$$\phi(z) = z - \frac{e^{-\mu z}}{\mu e^{-\mu z}} = z - \frac{1}{\mu}$$

**Step 2 — Reserve Price (1 mark):**

$\phi(r^*) = 0 \Rightarrow r^* = 1/\mu$. Exponential is regular since $\phi$ is strictly increasing.

**Step 3 — Optimal Mechanism (2 marks):**

- Allocate to the agent with the highest $v_i$ if $\max v_i \geq 1/\mu$.
- Charge the winner $\max\{1/\mu, \text{second highest value}\}$.
- This is a **second-price auction with reserve $1/\mu$**.

**Step 4 — Expected Revenue (Large-$n$ Limit) (2 marks):**

For $n$ i.i.d. exponential agents, by standard results, the expected revenue of the optimal mechanism is:
$$E[R_n] = \frac{n - 1}{\mu}$$

In the large-$n$ limit (normalized by $n$):
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{E[R_n]}{n} = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

This is precisely the **revenue from selling to a single agent with no competition**, which makes intuitive sense: with many agents, competition drives the price to the highest possible reserve level.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 marks]**
> **(a)** *Derive Myerson's payment formula for a single-parameter environment. Show all steps from the IC and IR constraints to the final expression.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Consider a single object allocation where agent $i$ wins the object if and only if her bid $b_i$ exceeds a critical value $z_i^* = 50$. Find the agent's payment and utility as functions of $b_i$. Use the Myerson formula to verify your answer.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**

The utility to agent $i$ with true value $v_i$ from report $\hat{v}_i$ (others report $v_{-i}$):
$$u_i(\hat{v}_i \mid v_i) = v_i \cdot x_i(\hat{v}_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(\hat{v}_i, v_{-i})$$

**Step 2 — IC and IR Constraints (1 mark):**

- **IC:** For all $v_i, \hat{v}_i$: $u_i(v_i \mid v_i) \geq u_i(\hat{v}_i \mid v_i)$.
- **IR:** $u_i(0 \mid v_i) \geq 0$. Combined with $x_i(0, v_{-i}) = 0$, this gives $p_i(0, v_{-i}) = 0$.

**Step 3 — Envelope Theorem (2 marks):**

Differentiating the IC constraint w.r.t. the report (treated as continuous) at the truth-telling point:
$$\frac{d}{d\hat{v}_i} u_i(\hat{v}_i \mid v_i)\bigg|_{\hat{v}_i = v_i} = x_i(v_i, v_{-i})$$

The intuition: at the optimum, the agent's marginal utility of reporting a slightly higher value equals her allocation probability (the "informational rent" derivative).

**Step 4 — Integration (1 mark):**

Integrating from $0$ to $v_i$ (using $u_i(0 \mid v_i) = 0$ from IR):
$$u_i(v_i \mid v_i) = \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Payment (2 marks):**

Substituting back $u_i(v_i \mid v_i) = v_i x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(v_i, v_{-i})$:
$$v_i x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$$

Solving:
$$p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = v_i \cdot x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$$

This is the **Myerson Payment Formula (Q.E.D.)**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Define the Allocation Rule (1 mark):**

$$x_i(v_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } v_i \geq 50 \\ 0 & \text{if } v_i < 50 \end{cases}$$

The critical value is $z_i^* = 50$. The allocation is a **step function** in $v_i$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Payment Using Myerson's Formula (3 marks):**

$$p_i(v_i) = v_i \cdot x_i(v_i) - \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z) dz$$

For $v_i < 50$: $x_i(v_i) = 0$ and $x_i(z) = 0$ for all $z \leq v_i < 50$, so:
$$p_i(v_i) = v_i \cdot 0 - \int_0^{v_i} 0 \, dz = 0$$

For $v_i \geq 50$: $x_i(v_i) = 1$ and $\int_0^{v_i} x_i(z) dz = \int_{50}^{v_i} 1 \, dz = v_i - 50$, so:
$$p_i(v_i) = v_i \cdot 1 - (v_i - 50) = 50$$

**Step 3 — Final Payment Function (1 mark):**

$$p_i(v_i) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } v_i < 50 \\ 50 & \text{if } v_i \geq 50 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Utility (2 marks):**

The agent's utility is:
$$u_i(v_i) = v_i \cdot x_i(v_i) - p_i(v_i) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } v_i < 50 \\ v_i - 50 & \text{if } v_i \geq 50 \end{cases}$$

This is the standard "option value" utility — the agent gains $v_i - 50$ by winning at the critical price of 50. The mechanism is strategy-proof: any agent with $v_i < 50$ has no incentive to bid above 50 (she would pay 50 and gain $v_i - 50 < 0$), and any agent with $v_i \geq 50$ has no incentive to bid above 50 (she would just pay more without changing her outcome).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Welfare and Revenue:** Students often write the *welfare-maximizing* allocation (highest value wins) when the question asks for the *revenue-maximizing* mechanism. These are different! Welfare maximizer = no reserve price; revenue maximizer = with reserve price.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Constant in Myerson's Payment:** The Myerson payment formula is unique *up to an additive constant*. The constant is determined by the IR condition $p_i(0) = 0$. Forgetting to apply IR loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing Monotonicity of $x_i$ vs. Monotonicity of $\phi_i$:** Myerson's Lemma requires $x_i$ to be monotone. The regularity condition requires $\phi_i$ to be monotone. These are different conditions on different functions!
>
> 4. **Wrong Sign in Virtual Valuation:** The virtual valuation is $\phi(z) = z - (1 - F(z))/f(z)$. Some textbooks use $\phi(z) = z - F(z)/f(z)$. Check the convention in your course materials.
>
> 5. **Skipping the IR Check:** When asked for an "optimal mechanism," always verify that it satisfies IR (i.e., the agent with $v = 0$ pays 0 and has non-negative utility).
>
> 6. **Not Specifying the Reserve Price Explicitly:** When asked for the optimal mechanism, you must explicitly state the reserve price $r^*$ and the rule for allocation in terms of $r^*$.
>
> 7. **Confusing "Allocation Rule" with "Mechanism":** The mechanism is a *pair* $(x, p)$. The allocation rule alone is not a mechanism. Always specify both.
>
> 8. **Misapplying Ironing:** Ironing is needed only for *irregular* distributions. For the standard examples (uniform, exponential), no ironing is required.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**
>
> **Affine Maximizers:**
> - [x] An allocation rule of the form $\arg\max_o \{\alpha_o + \sum_i w_i v_i x_i(o)\}$.
> - [x] **Rochet's Theorem:** Implementable $\iff$ affine maximizer.
> - [x] Special case $w_i = 1, \alpha_o = 0$: welfare maximizer (efficient).
> - [x] In single object setting: $i^*(v) = \arg\max_i \{\alpha_i + w_i v_i\}$.
>
> **Single Object Allocation:**
> - [x] $n$ agents, single indivisible item, private values $v_i \in [0, \bar{v}_i]$.
> - [x] Feasibility: $\sum_i x_i(v) \leq 1$.
> - [x] Goal: maximize welfare or revenue subject to IC + IR.
> - [x] Standard analogy: art auction, spectrum auction, government procurement.
>
> **Myerson's Lemma:**
> - [x] **Theorem:** $x$ is implementable (with single-parameter agents) $\iff$ $x_i$ is monotone in $v_i$ for all $i$.
> - [x] **Payment formula:** $p_i(v_i, v_{-i}) = v_i x_i(v_i, v_{-i}) - \int_0^{v_i} x_i(z, v_{-i}) dz$.
> - [x] **IR pinning:** $p_i(0, v_{-i}) = 0$.
> - [x] **Geometric meaning:** $p_i$ is the area between the allocation curve and its bounding rectangle.
> - [x] **Step function case:** $p_i = z_i^* \cdot \mathbb{1}[v_i \geq z_i^*]$ (winner pays the critical value).
> - [x] **Proof technique:** Envelope theorem + integration.
>
> **Optimal Mechanism Design:**
> - [x] **Virtual valuation:** $\phi_i(z) = z - (1 - F_i(z))/f_i(z)$.
> - [x] **Regularity:** $\phi_i$ is strictly increasing $\iff$ distribution is regular.
> - [x] **Myerson's Optimal Auction:** Allocate to $\arg\max_i \phi_i(v_i)$ with $\phi_{i^*} \geq 0$; charge critical value.
> - [x] **Reserve price (i.i.d. regular):** $r^*$ satisfies $\phi(r^*) = 0$.
> - [x] **Equivalent to:** Second-price auction with reserve price $r^*$.
> - [x] **Ironing:** Required for irregular distributions; replace $\phi_i$ by the slope of its convex hull.
> - [x] **Expected revenue:** $E[R] = E[\sum_i \phi_i(v_i) x_i(v)]$ (virtual surplus formula).
>
> **Key Standard Examples to Memorize:**
> - [x] **Uniform$[0,1]$:** $\phi(z) = 2z - 1$, $r^* = 1/2$.
> - [x] **Exponential$(\mu)$:** $\phi(z) = z - 1/\mu$, $r^* = 1/\mu$.
> - [x] **Uniform$[a, b]$:** $\phi(z) = 2z - (a + b)$, $r^* = (a+b)/2$ (midpoint).
>
> **Engineering Applications to Mention in Answers:**
> - [x] Spectrum auctions (FCC, India DOT).
> - [x] Sponsored search (Google, Bing ad auctions).
> - [x] Cloud resource allocation.
> - [x] Electricity market design.
> - [x] Blockchain transaction fee mechanisms.
> - [x] Government procurement reverse auctions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single and Multi-agent Optimal Mechanism Design, Examples of Optimal Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST756 - GAME THEORY AND MECHANISM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single and Multi-agent Optimal Mechanism Design, Examples of Optimal Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Mechanism Design?

**Mechanism Design** is the sub-field of game theory and economics that asks the reverse question of classical game theory. Instead of analyzing the equilibria *given* the rules of a game, mechanism design starts from a desired social outcome and works *backwards* to construct the rules (the "mechanism") that incentivize self-interested agents to achieve that outcome.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Mechanism Design is "reverse game theory." It is the art of designing economic institutions (rules, prices, allocations, penalties) such that rational, self-interested agents, pursuing their own private utilities, will collectively produce an outcome that the designer desires — typically maximizing social welfare, expected revenue, or some other objective.*

Formally, a **mechanism** $\mathcal{M}$ is a tuple:
$$\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{X}, \mathcal{T}, g)$$

where:
- $\mathcal{N} = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ — the set of **agents** (players)
- $\mathcal{X}$ — the set of **social alternatives** (allocations, outcomes)
- $\mathcal{T} = T_1 \times T_2 \times \cdots \times T_n$ — the set of **type profiles** (each agent $i$ has a private type $\theta_i \in T_i$)
- $g : \mathcal{T} \to \Delta(\mathcal{X})$ — the **social choice function** mapping reported type profiles to probability distributions over outcomes

> [!NOTE]
> The **direct revelation mechanism** is a special case where the action space equals the type space: $A_i = T_i$. Agents simply announce their type, and the designer uses the report to compute the outcome.

### 1.2 Single-Agent vs. Multi-Agent Mechanism Design

| Dimension | **Single-Agent Setting** | **Multi-Agent Setting** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of principals | One decision-maker | Multiple competing or cooperating agents |
| Typical objective | Monopoly profit / welfare extraction | Auctions, market design, public goods |
| Core challenge | Hidden preferences of one agent | Strategic interaction + hidden preferences |
| Key theorem | Bayesian optimal pricing (Baron-Myerson) | Myerson's optimal auction (1981) |
| Example | Insurance pricing, monopoly screening | FCC spectrum auction, Google ad auction |

### 1.3 Optimal Mechanism Design — Conceptual Intuition

**Analogy 1: The Bakery Analogy (Single-Agent)**

Imagine a single baker who knows your taste preferences for cake sweetness (your *type*), but you don't reveal them honestly — you might exaggerate a craving for cheap chocolate to get a discount. The baker (the *designer*) must design a **menu of cake options** (a *mechanism*) — different combinations of price and sweetness — such that:
1. You voluntarily choose the option that best matches your *true* preference (**incentive compatibility**).
2. You prefer participating to walking away (**individual rationality / participation**).
3. The baker's expected profit is maximized.

This is precisely the **optimal screening problem** for a single agent.

**Analogy 2: The Auction House (Multi-Agent)**

A government wants to sell a radio spectrum license. Multiple telecom companies (agents) have private valuations. The seller (designer) must design **auction rules** — bid format, payment, allocation — so that the company that values the spectrum most wins, the seller collects the highest revenue, and everyone bids truthfully. Myerson's 1981 Nobel-Prize-winning result tells us exactly how to do this.

### 1.4 The Designer's Constraints (KTU-Standard)

Any optimal mechanism must satisfy three properties:

1. **Incentive Compatibility (IC):** Each agent maximizes utility by reporting $\theta_i$ truthfully.
2. **Individual Rationality (IR):** Each agent gets non-negative expected utility by participating (ex-post or interim, depending on the model).
3. **Allocation Feasibility:** The output $g(\theta)$ must lie in $\mathcal{X}$ for every $\theta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Revelation Principle (Gibbard–Satterthwaite / Myerson)**
> *Any social choice function achievable by any mechanism can also be achieved by a **truthful direct revelation mechanism**, provided the agents' utilities satisfy quasi-linearity.* This is the cornerstone of mechanism design — we can restrict attention to direct mechanisms without loss of generality.

### 1.5 The Quasi-Linear Utility Assumption (Used Universally in KTU)

For an agent $i$ with monetary payment $t_i$ and received allocation $x_i$:

$$u_i(x_i, \theta_i; t_i) = v_i(x_i, \theta_i) - t_i$$

This is called **quasi-linear utility** — utility is linear in money, allowing the designer to trade off money and allocations cleanly. The **virtual valuation** machinery developed by Myerson relies fundamentally on this assumption.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Mechanism Design Workflow

The mechanism designer's pipeline can be decomposed into four logical stages:

1. **Model the Environment** — Define the type space $T_i$, prior distribution $F_i(\theta_i)$ over types, and the designer's objective $W(\theta)$ (e.g., expected revenue, expected social welfare).
2. **Specify Constraints** — Enforce **IC** and **IR** using utility-theoretic conditions.
3. **Optimize** — Solve the designer's objective subject to the constraints (typically a linear program over allocation rules).
4. **Implement** — Apply the **Revelation Principle** to convert any optimal indirect mechanism into a direct one.

### 2.2 Key Theoretical Components

#### A. Type Space and Prior

- Each agent $i$'s type $\theta_i \in T_i \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is **private information**.
- Types are drawn i.i.d. (or from a known joint distribution) $F(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n)$.
- For a single agent, the type is often the *valuation* $v \in [\underline{v}, \bar{v}]$ with CDF $F(v)$ and density $f(v) > 0$.

#### B. Direct Revelation Mechanism

- Agent reports $\hat{\theta}_i \in T_i$.
- Designer applies allocation rule $x(\hat{\theta})$ and payment rule $t(\hat{\theta})$.
- Agent's utility: $U_i(\hat{\theta}_i, \theta_i) = \theta_i \cdot x(\hat{\theta}) - t(\hat{\theta})$.

#### C. Bayesian Incentive Compatibility (BIC)

Truth-telling $\hat{\theta}_i = \theta_i$ must be a **Bayesian Nash equilibrium** of the reporting game:

$$U_i(\theta_i, \theta_i) \geq U_i(\hat{\theta}_i, \theta_i) \quad \forall \, \theta_i, \hat{\theta}_i \in T_i$$

#### D. Interim Individual Rationality (IR)

Each agent prefers participating to an outside option of zero utility:

$$U_i(\theta_i, \theta_i) \geq 0 \quad \forall \, \theta_i \in T_i$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quasi-linear utility | $u_i = v_i(x_i, \theta_i) - t_i$ | Foundation of mechanism design |
| 2 | Bayesian IC (truth-telling) | $\mathbb{E}_{\theta_{-i}}[U_i(\theta_i, \theta_i)] \geq \mathbb{E}_{\theta_{-i}}[U_i(\hat{\theta}_i, \theta_i)]$ | Direct mechanism condition |
| 3 | Monotonicity (IC $\Rightarrow$ Monotone allocation) | $x(\theta_i)$ non-decreasing in $\theta_i$ | Myerson's Lemma (1981) |
| 4 | Payment identity (Myerson) | $t(\theta_i) = \theta_i \cdot x(\theta_i) - \int_{\underline{\theta}}^{\theta_i} x(s) \, ds + U_i(\underline{\theta}, \underline{\theta})$ | Determines payment from allocation |
| 5 | Virtual valuation (single agent) | $\psi(v) = v - \frac{1 - F(v)}{f(v)}$ | The "ironed" version handles non-monotonicity |
| 6 | Ironed virtual valuation | $\bar{\psi}(v)$ — convex hull of $\psi$ from above | Used when $\psi$ is non-monotone |
| 7 | Expected revenue (Myerson) | $\mathbb{E}[R] = \mathbb{E}[\bar{\psi}(\theta) \cdot x(\theta)]$ | Revenue = expected ironed virtual surplus |
| 8 | Expected social welfare | $\mathbb{E}[W] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_i v_i(\theta_i) \cdot x_i(\theta)\right]$ | Vickrey outcome when types independent |
| 9 | Monopoly price (single agent) | $p^* = \arg\max_v \, (1 - F(v)) \cdot v$ | Highest valuation not priced out |
| 10 | Optimal allocation rule | $x^*(\theta) = \mathbf{1}\{\bar{\psi}(\theta) \geq 0\}$ | Allocate iff ironed virtual value non-negative |
| 11 | Expected utility of lowest type | $U_i(\underline{\theta}) = 0$ | IR binding at the bottom type |
| 12 | Allocation probability range | $x(\theta_i) \in [0,1]$ | Probabilistic allocation |
| 13 | Single-agent objective | $\max_x \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} \psi(v) \cdot x(v) f(v) \, dv$ | Subject to $x \in [0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight — Myerson's Lemma (1981, Nobel 2007)**
> In a single-parameter environment, a social choice function is implementable (i.e., admits a BIC direct mechanism) **if and only if the allocation rule is monotone non-decreasing in the agent's type**. Once the allocation rule is fixed, the payment rule is uniquely determined (up to a constant) by the payment identity above. This collapses an infinite-dimensional mechanism design problem into choosing a single monotone function.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spectrum Auctions (FCC, 3G/4G/5G):** Myerson's optimal auction generated tens of billions in US government revenue.
- **Online Ad Auctions (Google, Meta):** Generalized second-price (GSP) and Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) are practical implementations.
- **Electricity Market Design:** Locational marginal pricing uses mechanism design to clear competitive wholesale markets.
- **AI Alignment & Recommender Systems:** Optimal mechanism design now informs pricing of digital goods, differential privacy auctions, and federated learning incentives.

### 2.5 When Standard Optimal Design Breaks Down

- **Non-quasi-linear utilities** (e.g., risk-averse agents) — Rochet-Choné (1998) generalizes screening.
- **Multi-dimensional types** — No closed-form equivalent of Myerson's Lemma; computational methods (MNL, automated mechanism design) required.
- **Budget constraints** — Laffont-Marx (2002) with limited liability.
- **Correlated types / Common values** — Wilson's doctrine, link principle.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Single-Agent Bayesian Optimal Pricing (Baron-Myerson 1982)

**Setup:** A monopolist seller (designer) faces a single buyer with private valuation $v \sim F(v)$ on support $[\underline{v}, \bar{v}]$. The seller offers a quantity schedule $q(v)$ and price schedule $p(v)$. The buyer's utility is $U(v) = v \cdot q(v) - p(v)$.

**Step 1: Write the buyer's incentive compatibility (BIC) condition.**

For the buyer to report $v$ truthfully when true type is $v$, the derivative condition from envelope theorem gives:

$$\frac{dU(v)}{dv} = q(v)$$

**Step 2: Apply the Envelope Theorem.**

The envelope theorem in mechanism design states that for a utility function maximized at truth-telling:

$$U(v) = U(\underline{v}) + \int_{\underline{v}}^{v} q(s) \, ds$$

**Step 3: Apply Individual Rationality.**

For an agent to participate voluntarily:

$$U(\underline{v}) \geq 0 \implies U(\underline{v}) = 0 \text{ (binding at lowest type)}$$

Therefore:

$$U(v) = \int_{\underline{v}}^{v} q(s) \, ds$$

**Step 4: Express the seller's expected revenue.**

The seller's revenue is the expected payment:

$$\mathbb{E}[R] = \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} p(v) f(v) \, dv$$

Using the buyer's utility identity $p(v) = v \cdot q(v) - U(v)$:

$$p(v) = v \cdot q(v) - \int_{\underline{v}}^{v} q(s) \, ds$$

**Step 5: Substitute into expected revenue.**

$$\mathbb{E}[R] = \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} \left[ v \cdot q(v) - \int_{\underline{v}}^{v} q(s) \, ds \right] f(v) \, dv$$

**Step 6: Apply integration by parts to the second term.**

Let me apply Fubini's theorem. Define $G(v) = \int_{\underline{v}}^{v} q(s) \, ds$. Then:

$$\int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} G(v) f(v) \, dv = \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} \int_{\underline{v}}^{v} q(s) f(v) \, ds \, dv = \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} q(s) \int_{s}^{\bar{v}} f(v) \, dv \, ds$$

Using $1 - F(s) = \int_{s}^{\bar{v}} f(v) \, dv$:

$$\int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} G(v) f(v) \, dv = \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} q(s) [1 - F(s)] \, ds$$

**Step 7: Substitute back.**

$$\mathbb{E}[R] = \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} v \cdot q(v) f(v) \, dv - \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} q(v) [1 - F(v)] \, dv$$

Factor $q(v)$:

$$\mathbb{E}[R] = \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} q(v) \left[ v - \frac{1 - F(v)}{f(v)} \right] f(v) \, dv$$

**Step 8: Define the Virtual Valuation.**

$$\boxed{\psi(v) = v - \frac{1 - F(v)}{f(v)}}$$

This is the **Myerson virtual valuation** of type $v$. The seller's expected revenue becomes:

$$\mathbb{E}[R] = \int_{\underline{v}}^{\bar{v}} q(v) \, \psi(v) f(v) \, dv$$

**Step 9: Solve the pointwise optimization.**

For each $v$, the seller chooses $q(v) \in \{0, 1\}$ (sell or don't sell) to maximize $q(v) \cdot \psi(v)$:

$$q^*(v) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \psi(v) \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \psi(v) < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 10: Iron the virtual valuation if non-monotone.**

If $\psi(v)$ is non-monotone in $v$, replace it with its **ironed version** $\bar{\psi}(v)$ — the smallest concave function lying above $\psi$ — and apply the same rule with $\bar{\psi}$ in place of $\psi$. This is the **Ironed Virtual Valuation Theorem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — Uniform Distribution**
> Let $v \sim U[0, 1]$, so $F(v) = v$ and $f(v) = 1$. Then:
> $$\psi(v) = v - \frac{1 - v}{1} = 2v - 1$$
> This is monotone, so $\bar{\psi} = \psi$. The seller sells iff $2v - 1 \geq 0 \iff v \geq 1/2$. The **monopoly price is $\boxed{p^* = 1/2}$**, and the **expected revenue is $\boxed{\mathbb{E}[R] = 1/4}$**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Myerson's Optimal Auction (Multi-Agent)

**Setup:** A single indivisible item is sold to $n$ risk-neutral bidders. Bidder $i$'s valuation $v_i \in [0, 1]$ is drawn i.i.d. from $F$ with density $f$.

**Step 1: Mechanism output.** A direct mechanism outputs $(x_1, \ldots, x_n, t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ where $\sum_i x_i \leq 1$.

**Step 2: Expected revenue (apply Myerson's payment identity to each agent).**

By symmetry, bidder $i$'s expected revenue contribution is:

$$\mathbb{E}[R_i] = \int_{0}^{1} \left[ v_i \cdot x_i(v_i) - \int_{0}^{v_i} x_i(s) \, ds \right] f(v_i) \, dv_i$$

After integration by parts (Fubini):

$$\mathbb{E}[R_i] = \int_{0}^{1} x_i(v_i) \, \psi(v_i) f(v_i) \, dv_i$$

Total expected revenue (sum over $i$, exchanging sum and integral):

$$\mathbb{E}[R] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \int_{0}^{1} x_i(v_i) \, \psi(v_i) f(v_i) \, dv_i$$

**Step 3: Allocation feasibility constraint.**

The sum of allocation probabilities cannot exceed 1:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i(v_i) \leq 1 \quad \forall (v_1, \ldots, v_n)$$

**Step 4: Solve the relaxed pointwise problem.**

Dropping the feasibility constraint for a moment, the optimal unconstrained rule for bidder $i$ is $x_i(v_i) = 1$ if $\psi(v_i) > 0$, else $0$. When $\psi$ is strictly increasing (regular distribution), this is the **second-price auction with reserve price** $r^* = \psi^{-1}(0)$:

- Bidders with $v_i \geq r^*$ are "in."
- The highest such bidder wins.
- The winner pays the **second-highest bid** or the **reserve price**, whichever is higher.

**Step 5: Verify feasibility.**

For any profile of bids, at most one bidder has the highest value $\geq r^*$, so the allocation constraint is satisfied.

**Step 6: State the Optimal Auction Theorem.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Myerson's Optimal Auction Theorem (1981)**
> *For bidders with i.i.d. regular valuations, the expected-revenue-maximizing auction is:*
> 1. *Set a reserve price $r^* = \psi^{-1}(0)$.*
> 2. *Allocate the item to the highest bidder if her bid exceeds $r^*$.*
> 3. *Charge the winner the maximum of the second-highest bid and $r^*$ (i.e., the smallest bid that would still win).*

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Vickrey's Second-Price Auction**
> When the reserve is set to $r^* = 0$ (i.e., the distribution is such that $\psi(v) \geq 0$ for all $v$, like $F(v) = v^2$ on $[0,1]$), the optimal auction collapses to the standard Vickrey second-price auction. This is a celebrated result: **Vickrey is revenue-optimal only for specific distributions**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Single-Agent (Monopoly) | Multi-Agent (Auction) |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | $\max \mathbb{E}[p]$ | $\max \mathbb{E}[R]$ |
| **IC variable** | $q(v)$ quantity schedule | $x_i(v_i)$ allocation rule |
| **Payment identity** | $p(v) = v q(v) - \int q$ | $t_i(v) = v_i x_i - \int x_i$ |
| **Virtual valuation** | $\psi(v) = v - (1-F)/f$ | $\psi(v_i)$ same formula, per agent |
| **Optimal rule** | Sell iff $\bar{\psi}(v) \geq 0$ | Allocate to highest $\bar{\psi}$ bidder |
| **Format** | Quantity discount schedule | Auction with reserve + second price |
| **When IR binds** | Lowest type $v = \underline{v}$ | Lowest type $v_i = \underline{v}$ |

### 3.4 Example of Ironing — When $\psi(v)$ is Non-Monotone

Suppose $v$ is distributed with CDF $F(v) = v^2$ on $[0,1]$, so $f(v) = 2v$. Then:

$$\psi(v) = v - \frac{1 - v^2}{2v} = v - \frac{1}{2v} + \frac{v}{2} = \frac{3v}{2} - \frac{1}{2v}$$

The derivative $\psi'(v) = \frac{3}{2} + \frac{1}{2v^2} > 0$, so $\psi$ is strictly increasing. **No ironing required**.

In contrast, if $F$ is a bimodal mixture (e.g., 50% low-type, 50% high-type), $\psi$ is typically non-monotone, and the ironed version is essential. The designer pools types in intervals where $\psi$ dips.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define the Revelation Principle. Why is it central to mechanism design?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Revelation Principle** states that for any mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ and any equilibrium strategy profile $\sigma^*$ of $\mathcal{M}$, there exists a **truthful direct revelation mechanism** $\mathcal{M}'$ in which truthfully reporting one's type is an equilibrium, and the outcome of $\mathcal{M}'$ (when agents play truthfully) is identical to the outcome of $\mathcal{M}$ under $\sigma^*$.

It is central to mechanism design because it allows the designer to **restrict attention to direct mechanisms** without loss of generality. Instead of searching over an arbitrarily large space of indirect mechanisms and equilibrium concepts, the designer can simply choose a direct social choice function satisfying incentive compatibility. This collapses an intractable search into a constrained optimization problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often state that the Revelation Principle "makes truthful reporting optimal." It does **not** make truthful reporting uniquely optimal in *every* mechanism — it only says that for *any equilibrium* of *any* mechanism, there exists a direct mechanism in which *truth-telling is also an equilibrium* yielding the same outcome. The key word is "equivalent outcome," not "uniquely truthful."

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the virtual valuation $\psi(v)$ for a distribution $F$. Explain the role of regularity.*

**Model Answer:**

For a valuation $v$ drawn from a distribution with CDF $F$ and density $f > 0$, the **virtual valuation** is:

$$\psi(v) = v - \frac{1 - F(v)}{f(v)}$$

This represents the **marginal contribution of type $v$ to expected revenue**, properly accounting for the information rent that the seller must surrender to satisfy incentive compatibility.

A distribution is **regular** if $\psi(v)$ is **strictly increasing** in $v$. For regular distributions, the ironed virtual valuation $\bar{\psi}(v) = \psi(v)$, and the optimal mechanism is non-trivial: sell only to types with $\psi(v) \geq 0$. For non-regular distributions, $\psi$ is non-monotone, and the designer must apply **ironing** (replacing $\psi$ by its convex hull from above) to construct the optimal mechanism, which may involve **pooling** certain type intervals.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question in the End Semester Examination offers an **internal choice**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full 14 marks, with sub-parts typically 7 + 7). Both alternatives below are fully worked out.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECST756, Module 4]**

> A monopolist sells a single product to a single buyer. The buyer's valuation $v$ is uniformly distributed on $[0, 1]$. The seller's marginal cost is $c = 0$.

**(a)** *Derive the Bayesian incentive-compatible (BIC) direct mechanism that maximizes the seller's expected revenue. Compute the optimal quantity rule $q(v)$ and the price schedule $p(v)$.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compute the seller's expected revenue and the buyer's expected surplus at the optimum. Compare with the first-best (full-information) outcome.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Compute the virtual valuation.**

For $v \sim U[0,1]$, $F(v) = v$ and $f(v) = 1$:

$$\psi(v) = v - \frac{1 - F(v)}{f(v)} = v - (1 - v) = 2v - 1$$

This is strictly increasing, so $\bar{\psi}(v) = \psi(v) = 2v - 1$.

**Step 2: Set up the expected revenue expression.**

$$\mathbb{E}[R] = \int_{0}^{1} q(v) \psi(v) f(v) \, dv = \int_{0}^{1} q(v)(2v - 1) \, dv$$

**Step 3: Solve the pointwise optimization.**

For each $v$, choose $q(v) \in \{0, 1\}$ to maximize $q(v)(2v-1)$:

- If $2v - 1 > 0$ (i.e., $v > 0.5$), set $q(v) = 1$.
- If $2v - 1 < 0$ (i.e., $v < 0.5$), set $q(v) = 0$.
- At $v = 0.5$, indifferent — set $q(0.5) = 1$ (or $0$, measure-zero event).

Thus the optimal **quantity rule** is:

$$q^*(v) = \begin{cases} 1 & v \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & v < 0.5 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Compute the price schedule using the payment identity.**

By Myerson's payment identity with $U(\underline{v}) = U(0) = 0$:

$$p(v) = v \cdot q(v) - \int_{0}^{v} q(s) \, ds$$

For $v \in [0, 0.5]$: $q(v) = 0$ and $q(s) = 0$ for $s \in [0, v]$, so $p(v) = 0$.

For $v \in [0.5, 1]$: $q(v) = 1$ and $q(s) = 0$ for $s \in [0, 0.5]$, $q(s) = 1$ for $s \in [0.5, v]$:

$$p(v) = v - \int_{0.5}^{v} 1 \, ds = v - (v - 0.5) = 0.5$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{p^*(v) = \begin{cases} 0.5 & v \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & v < 0.5 \end{cases}}$$

**Interpretation:** The seller offers a take-it-or-leave-it price of $0.5$. The buyer buys if and only if her valuation $v \geq 0.5$. **(7 marks awarded for the complete derivation.)**

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 5: Compute expected revenue.**

Only buyers with $v \geq 0.5$ purchase, and each pays $p = 0.5$:

$$\mathbb{E}[R^*] = 0.5 \cdot P(v \geq 0.5) = 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 0.25$$

**Step 6: Compute expected buyer surplus.**

The buyer's surplus is $v - 0.5$ when she buys, and $0$ otherwise:

$$\mathbb{E}[S_{\text{buyer}}] = \int_{0.5}^{1} (v - 0.5) \, dv = \left[ \frac{(v-0.5)^2}{2} \right]_{0.5}^{1} = \frac{(0.5)^2}{2} = 0.125$$

**Step 7: First-best (full-information) benchmark.**

If the seller knew $v$ exactly, the efficient outcome is to sell whenever $v \geq 0$ (since cost is $0$). The seller would price-discriminate perfectly, charging $p = v$ to each buyer. **Expected revenue = $\mathbb{E}[v] = 0.5$**, **buyer surplus = 0**, **total surplus = 0.5**.

**Step 8: Comparison.**

| Outcome | Revenue | Buyer Surplus | Total Surplus |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-best (full info) | 0.500 | 0.000 | 0.500 |
| Optimal BIC (asymmetric info) | 0.250 | 0.125 | 0.375 |
| **Efficiency loss** | – | – | **0.125 (25%)** |

The seller's revenue is **halved** under asymmetric information. The total surplus drops by 0.125 because buyers with $v \in [0, 0.5]$ are inefficiently excluded (they value the good above cost, but the seller cannot extract their surplus without scaring off the higher types — this is the **no-distortion-at-the-top** principle). **(7 marks awarded for the comparative analysis.)**

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECST756, Module 4]**

> Two bidders compete in a single-item auction. Valuations $v_1, v_2$ are drawn i.i.d. from $F(v) = v$ on $[0, 1]$.

**(a)** *Apply Myerson's optimal auction framework to derive the optimal reserve price and the expected revenue of the optimal auction.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the expected revenue of the optimal auction with: (i) the standard Vickrey second-price auction with no reserve, and (ii) a first-price auction with no reserve (assuming a symmetric Bayes-Nash equilibrium with truthful bidding). Discuss which auction is revenue-superior and why.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Compute the virtual valuation.**

With $F(v) = v$ and $f(v) = 1$:

$$\psi(v) = v - \frac{1 - v}{1} = 2v - 1$$

Monotone increasing ⇒ $\bar{\psi}(v) = \psi(v) = 2v - 1$.

**Step 2: Find the optimal reserve price.**

The reserve $r^*$ is the unique $v$ such that $\psi(v) = 0$:

$$2r^* - 1 = 0 \implies r^* = 0.5$$

**Step 3: State the optimal auction.**

Allocate to the highest bidder $i^*$ if her bid exceeds $r^* = 0.5$; charge her the maximum of the second-highest bid and $0.5$.

**Step 4: Compute expected revenue.**

Let $V_{(1)} \leq V_{(2)}$ be the order statistics. With $n = 2$ i.i.d. $U[0,1]$:

$$V_{(1)} \sim \text{Beta}(1,2), \quad V_{(2)} \sim \text{Beta}(2,1)$$

The item is sold iff $V_{(2)} \geq 0.5$. The winner pays $\max(V_{(1)}, 0.5)$.

$$\mathbb{E}[R^*] = \mathbb{E}\left[ \max(V_{(1)}, 0.5) \cdot \mathbf{1}\{V_{(2)} \geq 0.5\} \right]$$

**Case 1:** $V_{(1)} \geq 0.5$ and $V_{(2)} \geq 0.5$. Then payment is $V_{(1)}$.

$$P(V_{(1)} \geq 0.5, V_{(2)} \geq 0.5) = P(\text{both} \geq 0.5) = 0.5^2 = 0.25$$

Conditional on both $\geq 0.5$, the joint distribution of $(V_{(1)}, V_{(2)})$ given both $\geq 0.5$ has the same order-statistic ratio. The expected payment is:

$$\mathbb{E}[V_{(1)} \mid V_{(1)} \geq 0.5, V_{(2)} \geq 0.5] = \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{1 + V_{(1)}'}{2} \mid V_{(1)}', V_{(2)}' \in U[0.5, 1]\right]$$

By symmetry, $V_{(1)}' \sim U[0.5, 1]$ marginally, so $\mathbb{E}[V_{(1)}'] = 0.75$. Hence expected payment in this case = $0.75 \times 0.25 = 0.1875$.

**Case 2:** $V_{(1)} < 0.5 \leq V_{(2)}$. Payment is $0.5$. Probability:

$$P(V_{(1)} < 0.5, V_{(2)} \geq 0.5) = P(V_{(2)} \geq 0.5) - P(V_{(1)} \geq 0.5) = 0.75 - 0.25 = 0.50$$

Wait — let me recompute. $P(V_{(2)} \geq 0.5) = 1 - P(V_{(2)} < 0.5) = 1 - (0.5)^2 = 0.75$. $P(V_{(1)} \geq 0.5) = (0.5)^2 = 0.25$.

So Case 2 probability = $0.75 - 0.25 = 0.50$. Expected payment in this case = $0.5 \times 0.50 = 0.25$.

**Case 3:** Both $< 0.5$. No sale, revenue $= 0$. Probability = $0.25$.

**Total:** $\mathbb{E}[R^*] = 0 + 0.25 + 0.1875 = \boxed{0.4375}$ **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Vickrey Second-Price Auction, No Reserve (Reserve = 0):**

The winner pays the second-highest bid. With $U[0,1]$ valuations, the item is sold with probability $1$ (since at least one bidder has $v \geq 0$ almost surely). The expected revenue equals the expected second-highest order statistic:

$$\mathbb{E}[R_{\text{Vickrey}}] = \mathbb{E}[V_{(1)}] = \frac{1}{3}$$

Verification: For 2 i.i.d. $U[0,1]$, $\mathbb{E}[V_{(1)}] = \frac{1}{n+1} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$.

**(ii) First-Price Auction, No Reserve, Truthful Bidding:**

Truthful bidding in a first-price auction is **not** a Nash equilibrium (it would give zero surplus to the winner), but if we *impose* truthful bidding as a hypothetical baseline, the revenue equals $\mathbb{E}[V_{(2)}]$ when the highest bidder wins. The actual Bayes-Nash equilibrium of the first-price auction with 2 bidders has a bidding strategy $b(v) = v/2$ (for $U[0,1]$), giving expected revenue:

$$\mathbb{E}[R_{\text{FPA}}] = \mathbb{E}[V_{(1)}] = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

(by the **Revenue Equivalence Theorem**, both first- and second-price auctions yield the same expected revenue under the standard conditions).

**Comparison Table:**

| Auction | Reserve | Expected Revenue |
|---|---|---|
| Vickrey (no reserve) | $0$ | $0.333$ |
| First-price (BNE, no reserve) | $0$ | $0.333$ |
| **Myerson optimal (with reserve $0.5$)** | $0.5$ | $\mathbf{0.4375}$ |

**Discussion:** The Myerson optimal auction generates **strictly more revenue** ($0.4375 > 0.333$) than either standard format. The reason: by excluding low-valuation bidders (those with $v < 0.5$), the seller avoids "wasting" the item on a buyer who values it little, while the reserve price itself extracts additional surplus from the marginal winner. The Vickrey auction fails to do this because it always allocates the item to the highest bidder regardless of how small that bid is. **(7 marks awarded for the comparison and discussion.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes**
> 1. **Forgetting the Fubini step:** Many students write the payment identity but skip the integration-by-parts step that converts it into virtual-valuation form. The transition $\int p(v) f(v) dv \to \int \psi(v) q(v) f(v) dv$ carries **3 of 7 marks** in derivation questions. Always show this step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing ironed vs. raw $\psi$:** If a question says "non-regular distribution," you **must** apply ironing. Simply writing $q^*(v) = \mathbf{1}\{\psi(v) \geq 0\}$ without checking monotonicity will cost 2 marks.
> 3. **Bidding strategy in first-price auctions:** Bidding $b(v) = v$ (truthfully) is **not** a Nash equilibrium in a first-price auction. The correct BNE for 2 bidders with $U[0,1]$ is $b(v) = v/2$. Confusing these will cost full marks on the comparison sub-question.
> 4. **Order statistics:** For 2 i.i.d. $U[0,1]$ bidders, the **highest** valuation has mean $2/3$, the **lowest** has mean $1/3$. Don't swap these.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ **Mechanism design = reverse game theory** — design rules to produce desired outcomes from self-interested agents.
- ✅ **Mechanism tuple:** $\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{X}, \mathcal{T}, g)$ — agents, outcomes, type space, social choice function.
- ✅ **Quasi-linear utility** $u_i = v_i x_i - t_i$ is the standard KTU assumption.
- ✅ **Three core constraints:** BIC (truth-telling is best), IR (participation is voluntary), Feasibility (allocations are valid).
- ✅ **Revelation Principle** lets us restrict to direct mechanisms — Nobel-level cornerstone.
- ✅ **Virtual valuation:** $\psi(v) = v - (1 - F(v))/f(v)$.
- ✅ **Myerson's Lemma (1981):** BIC $\iff$ monotone allocation rule. Payment is then uniquely determined.
- ✅ **Optimal rule:** Allocate iff $\bar{\psi}(v) \geq 0$ (use ironed version if $\psi$ is non-monotone).
- ✅ **Single-agent (Baron-Myerson 1982):** Offer a quantity-price menu; optimal is to sell at monopoly price $p^* = \arg\max (1 - F(v)) v$.
- ✅ **Multi-agent (Myerson 1981):** Second-price auction with optimal reserve $r^* = \psi^{-1}(0)$.
- ✅ **Regular distribution:** $\psi$ strictly increasing ⇒ no ironing needed. Examples: $U[0,1]$, $F(v) = v^2$.
- ✅ **Non-regular distribution:** $\psi$ non-monotone ⇒ apply ironing (convex hull from above) and pool types.
- ✅ **Revenue Equivalence Theorem:** Many standard auctions (Vickrey, first-price) yield the same expected revenue under identical assumptions — but Myerson's optimal auction with reserve is **strictly revenue-superior** when the distribution is regular and the reserve is positive.
- ✅ **Key special case:** For $U[0,1]$, optimal reserve is $0.5$, optimal revenue is $0.4375$ (vs. $0.333$ for Vickrey).
- ✅ **Information rent** is the surplus the designer must leave to the agent to ensure truth-telling.
- ✅ **No distortion at the top:** The highest type gets full information rent; distortions appear at the bottom.
- ✅ **Examples to remember for the exam:** Monopoly screening, FCC spectrum auctions, Google ad auctions, bilateral trading, public goods provision, kidney exchange.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip**
> When a question asks for an "optimal mechanism," always structure your answer in **four moves**: (1) Compute $\psi(v)$; (2) Check monotonicity / iron if needed; (3) State the optimal allocation rule; (4) Derive the payment schedule using the payment identity. Examiners award marks for each of these steps, so do not skip any.

---
